title: Analysis of Residuals
text:
NoteDownloads




Excel Workbook (all 3 lessons)
ACFT Data (CSV)







Define and compute residuals for a linear regression model and interpret them as prediction errors in context.
Construct and interpret residual plots (residuals vs. fitted values) to assess whether linear regression assumptions are reasonable.
Analyze the distribution of residuals using summary statistics and plots to evaluate model fit and error behavior.
Evaluate whether a linear regression model is appropriate for decision-making based on diagnostic evidence.





In Lessons 8–9, we built a regression model on 26 training soldiers, validated it on 11 test soldiers, and compared MAE. We have evidence the model generalizes.
But is a linear model actually the right choice? Maybe the relationship is curved and we just can’t see it from the scatter plot alone. This is what residual analysis tells us.




Recall from Lesson 9: every time we make a prediction, there is an error — the difference between what actually happened and what the model predicted. That error is called a residual:
\[e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\]

Positive residual (\(e_i > 0\)): The model underpredicted — the actual value was higher.
Negative residual (\(e_i < 0\)): The model overpredicted — the actual value was lower.

Looking at any single residual isn’t very informative. What matters is the pattern across all residuals. If the residuals behave like random noise — no patterns, no trends — our assumptions were reasonable. If we see structure, we have a problem (Reading 2.3, Section 1).




A residual plot graphs the residuals (\(e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\)) against the fitted values (\(\hat{y}_i\)), with a reference line at \(e = 0\).
If our assumptions are met, the residual plot should show a horizontal band of points scattered randomly around zero — no pattern, no trends, roughly even spread (Reading 2.3, Section 4).




The reading describes three problems that show up in residual plots. Here is what each one looks like:















ImportantReading a Residual Plot (Reading 2.3, Section 4)











What You See
What It Means
Which Assumption Is Violated




Random scatter around zero
Model is appropriate
None — assumptions satisfied


Curved pattern (U-shape or arch)
Relationship is nonlinear
Linearity


Fan shape (spread increases or decreases)
Errors are systematically larger for some predictions
Constant variance


Large isolated points far from zero
Potential outliers — investigate before proceeding
May indicate model issues









Now let’s look at the residual plot for our ACFT model. Does it pass the test?















TipCritique: What Do You See?




Curved pattern? No — the points don’t follow a U-shape or arch. Linearity assumption looks reasonable.
Fan shape? The spread looks roughly constant across the range of fitted values. Constant variance looks okay.
Outliers? There are a couple of larger residuals, but nothing dramatically far from the rest.
Overall: Random scatter around zero. The linear model appears appropriate for this data.







Beyond the residual plot, we check whether the residuals are roughly symmetric around zero (Reading 2.3, Section 6). This relates to the normality assumption.















TipCritique: What Do You See?




Centered near zero? Yes — the distribution is roughly centered on the red dashed line.
Roughly symmetric? Reasonably so — no severe skew in one direction.
Extreme outliers? No residuals are dramatically far from the rest.

The normality assumption appears reasonable.


The standard deviation of residuals (\(s_e\)) tells us the typical size of a prediction error:
\[s_e = 24.09 \text{ lbs}\]
This means a typical prediction is off by about 24.09 lbs in either direction.










ImportantResidual Analysis Summary











Assumption
What We Checked
Verdict




Linearity
Residual plot: no curved pattern
Reasonable


Independence
Study design — each soldier measured once
Reasonable


Constant variance
Residual plot: no fan shape
Reasonable


Normality
Histogram: roughly symmetric around zero
Reasonable



All four assumptions appear reasonable for our model. Combined with the validation results from Lesson 9 (similar training and test MAE), we have strong evidence that the linear model is appropriate for this data.






What happens when we try to force a linear model on nonlinear data?









The scatter plot on the left might look “okay” at first glance. But the residual plot reveals a clear curved pattern — the model systematically overpredicts in the middle and underpredicts at the ends. The linearity assumption is violated. A nonlinear model would be more appropriate (Lesson 11).
This is why we never skip residual analysis — the residual plot catches problems the scatter plot might hide.




Open the L10 - Residuals tab in the workbook:







NoteStep 1: Compute Fitted Values and Residuals



Use your slope and intercept from L9 (training model):

Fitted column: = intercept_cell + slope_cell * height_cell
Residual column: = actual_weight - fitted_value

Drag both formulas down for all 26 training soldiers.








NoteStep 2: Create a Residual Plot




Select the Fitted column and Residual column.
Go to Insert -> Chart -> XY (Scatter).
Adjust the axis bounds so the data fills the chart.
Add a horizontal reference line at \(e = 0\):

Add a new data series with two points: (min fitted, 0) and (max fitted, 0).
Format this series as a dashed line with no markers.

Label axes: “Fitted Values” on x-axis, “Residuals” on y-axis.









NoteStep 3: Create a Histogram of Residuals




Select the Residual column.
Go to Insert -> Chart -> Histogram.
Check: Is the distribution roughly symmetric around zero?









NoteStep 4: Critique Your Plots



Look at your residual plot and histogram and answer:

Do you see a curved pattern? (linearity concern)
Do you see a fan shape? (constant variance concern)
Are there any outliers? (unusually large residuals)
Is the histogram roughly symmetric around zero? (normality)
Based on all evidence (Lessons 8–10), is this model appropriate for predicting soldier weight?













ImportantKey Takeaways




Residuals (\(e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\)) are prediction errors. They are evidence for whether our model assumptions hold.
A residual plot (fitted values vs. residuals) is the primary diagnostic:

Random scatter = assumptions satisfied
Curved pattern = linearity violated
Fan shape = constant variance violated
Outliers = investigate

A histogram of residuals checks the normality assumption: look for roughly symmetric distribution around zero.
\(s_e\) measures the typical prediction error in the same units as \(y\).
A model can have good \(R^2\) and MAE and still fail residual analysis. Always check before using a model for decisions.

title: Linear Regression I
text:
NoteDownloads




Excel Workbook (all 3 lessons)
ACFT Data (CSV)







Explain the purpose of simple linear regression as a predictive modeling tool.
Identify situations where simple linear regression is an appropriate model.
Create a simple linear regression model in Excel using a training set.
Interpret the slope and intercept of a linear regression model in context. (moved from Lesson 9)
Interpret \(R^2\) for a linear regression model built on training data. (moved from Lesson 9)





In Lesson 7, you used the mean as a baseline predictor. Here is real ACFT data from a company in the 20th Engineer Brigade — can we predict a soldier’s Weight from their Height?









The mean predicts \(\bar{y} = 183.08\) lbs for every soldier — but the scatter shows a clear trend. Taller soldiers tend to weigh more. Can we do better?




We know the mean is too simple. But what model should we use? There are lots of curves we could draw through this data:









The data doesn’t curve back up or wiggle — it follows a roughly straight-line trend. Maybe a line is a better option:









This looks like it captures the trend well. But how do we find the best line? That is what simple linear regression does.










NoteJustifying the Choice of a Linear Model



Before fitting a model, we must justify our choice. Reading 2.2 describes four assumptions for simple linear regression:

Linearity: The relationship between \(x\) and \(y\) is approximately linear.
Independence: The observations are independent of each other.
Constant variance: The spread of \(y\) values is roughly the same for all values of \(x\).
Normality: The errors are approximately normally distributed.

We justify the linearity assumption by examining the scatter plot — does it show a reasonably straight-line pattern? For this data, the answer is yes.
The correlation between Height and Weight also supports this:


\[r = 0.7294\]






TipRecall from Reading 1.5



A strong positive correlation (close to \(+1\)) supports the existence of a linear relationship, but correlation alone does not prove linearity — we must always check the scatter plot. Correlation supports but does not replace visual inspection.






But which line? We could draw many different lines through this data:









Every one of these lines follows the same formula:
\[\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x\]
where \(\hat{\beta}_0\) is the intercept (where the line crosses \(y\) when \(x = 0\)) and \(\hat{\beta}_1\) is the slope (how much \(\hat{y}\) changes for each one-unit increase in \(x\)). Different choices of \(\hat{\beta}_0\) and \(\hat{\beta}_1\) give different lines.
But none of these lines hit every data point exactly. Each point has an error — the vertical distance between the actual value and the line:









So the real model is:
\[y = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x + \text{error}\]
The question is: which values of \(\hat{\beta}_0\) and \(\hat{\beta}_1\) make these errors as small as possible?










ImportantThe Least Squares Method



The “best” line is the one that minimizes the sum of squared errors (SSE):
\[\text{SSE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{\beta}_0 - \hat{\beta}_1 x_i)^2\]
The formulas for the least squares estimates are:
\[\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2} \qquad \qquad \hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}\]











The dashed gray lines show the errors (residuals) — the vertical distance between each data point and the line. Least squares finds the line that makes the total squared error as small as possible.






TipOur Model



From the training data:
\[\hat{y} = -233.2 + 6 \cdot x\]
where \(x\) = Height (in) and \(\hat{y}\) = predicted Weight (lbs).






Let’s build this live. The training data is in the workbook below:







NoteExcel Instructions: Trendline Method




Enter the training data in two columns: Height (\(x\)) and Weight (\(y\)).
Select cells B1:C38 (Height and Weight columns only) and go to Insert -> Chart -> XY (Scatter).
Adjust the axis bounds so the data fills the chart: double-click each axis -> Format Axis -> set the Minimum Bound (e.g., ~60 for Height, ~120 for Weight).
Click directly on one of the data points (blue dots) in the scatter plot — you should see selection handles appear on all the points.
Right-click on the selected data points -> Add Trendline. A Format Trendline pane will open on the right side of the screen.
Under Trendline Options, select Linear (it may already be selected by default).
Scroll down in the pane and check: Display Equation on chart.
Check: Display R-squared value on chart.
Close the pane.

The equation on the chart is your regression model.






The trendline gives us our model:
\[\hat{y} = -233.2 + 6 \cdot x\]
What do these numbers actually mean?






ImportantInterpreting the Slope and Intercept



Slope (\(\hat{\beta}_1 = 6\)): For every one-inch increase in height, the model predicts weight increases by about 6 lbs. This is the rate of change — how much \(\hat{y}\) changes per unit change in \(x\).
Intercept (\(\hat{\beta}_0 = -233.2\)): If a soldier were 0 inches tall, the model would predict a weight of -233.2 lbs. This is clearly nonsensical — the intercept is needed to position the line correctly, but it does not have a meaningful interpretation outside the range of the data.





The trendline also displayed an \(R^2\) value on the chart. What does it mean?
\[R^2 = 0.532\]






ImportantInterpreting R-squared



\(R^2\) measures the proportion of variation in \(y\) that is explained by the model. It ranges from 0 to 1.

\(R^2 = 0.532\) means that 53.2% of the variation in soldier weight is explained by the linear relationship with height.
The remaining 46.8% is due to other factors the model does not capture (fitness level, body composition, etc.).












ImportantKey Takeaways




Simple linear regression models the relationship between two numerical variables: \(\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x\).
The least squares method finds the line that minimizes SSE \(= \sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\).
In Excel, build the model using Trendline and verify with SLOPE and INTERCEPT.
Slope tells us the rate of change: how much \(\hat{y}\) changes per one-unit increase in \(x\).
Intercept positions the line but may not have a meaningful real-world interpretation.
\(R^2\) tells us what proportion of variation in \(y\) is explained by the model.

title: MA491: Data Science Practicum
text:
Course Description
MA491 is a capstone practicum for non-thesis cadets in the Department of Mathematical Sciences. The course takes a “cradle to grave” approach to data science: starting with bare hardware (Raspberry Pi), building up computing infrastructure and analytical tools, working through guided examples across multiple domains, and culminating in an individual end-to-end data science project.
The primary language is R, supported by command-line Linux skills, Git version control, and Quarto for reproducible reporting. Cadets will deliver a complete project including a GitHub repository, Quarto report, Shiny interactive application, and oral presentation.

title: MA491: Data Science Practicum
text:
Course Objectives
Upon completion of this course, cadets will be able to:

Set up and configure a computing environment from bare hardware
Acquire, clean, and wrangle data from multiple sources (files, APIs, web, databases)
Conduct exploratory data analysis and communicate findings visually
Apply statistical and machine learning methods (regression, classification, text analysis, spatial analysis, time series)
Build interactive web applications using Shiny
Use version control (Git/GitHub) and reproducible reporting (Quarto) in a professional workflow
Use AI tools (Claude, Copilot) responsibly and effectively as coding assistants
Execute an individual data science project from question formulation through presentation

title: MA491: Data Science Practicum
text:
Capstone Project Requirements
Each cadet will complete an individual end-to-end data science project. Final deliverables:

GitHub repository — clean directory structure, documented code, README, .gitignore, no secrets committed
Quarto report — introduction, data description, EDA, methods, results, conclusions
Shiny app — interactive dashboard with at least 2 inputs and 2 reactive outputs
10-minute presentation — communicate results to a non-technical audience; live demo of Shiny app
Reflection memo — 1-page reflection on what you learned, what you’d do differently, and which skills you’ll carry forward


Key Milestones



Milestone
Due




Project proposal (1 page)
Week 7


Data acquired & initial EDA
Week 13


Modeling complete & draft report
Week 14


Shiny app functional
Week 15


All deliverables finalized
Week 16


Presentation & final submission
Week 17

title: MA491: Data Science Practicum
text:
AI Policy
AI tools — Claude, GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, and others — are encouraged in this course. Data scientists in practice use every tool at their disposal, and you should too. Learning how to work effectively with AI is itself a course objective.
That said, using AI well is not the same as outsourcing your thinking. The standard is simple: you are the author of your work, and you must answer for every part of it.

Own every line. If you cannot explain why a line of code is there, what it does, and why it is correct, it should not be in your submission. AI can draft code quickly — but speed without understanding is a liability, not an asset.
Test relentlessly. AI-generated code can look right and be wrong. It can hallucinate function names, invert logic, or silently drop edge cases. You are responsible for verifying that your code runs correctly and produces correct results. “The AI wrote it” is not a defense.
You present, you defend. When you stand up to present your project or submit your report, the work is yours. You will be asked questions. You will need to justify your choices — your methods, your assumptions, your interpretation of results. If you cannot, it does not matter how polished the deliverable looks.
Use AI to learn, not to skip learning. The best use of AI in this course is as a thought partner: ask it to explain concepts, debug errors with you, suggest approaches you had not considered. The worst use is pasting a prompt and submitting the output without engaging your own judgment.

There are no restrictions on when or how often you use AI. The restriction is on presenting work you do not understand as your own. That is true with or without AI — it is simply the standard we hold as professionals.

title: MA491: Data Science Practicum
text:
Required Materials

Raspberry Pi (provided by the department)
Laptop with SSH client and web browser
GitHub account (free)
shinyapps.io account (free tier)


Recommended References

Wickham, Cetinkaya-Rundel, & Grolemund. R for Data Science (2nd ed.). Available free at r4ds.hadley.nz
Wickham. ggplot2: Elegant Graphics for Data Analysis (3rd ed.). Available free at ggplot2-book.org
Silge & Robinson. Text Mining with R. Available free at tidytextmining.com
Hyndman & Athanasopoulos. Forecasting: Principles and Practice (3rd ed.). Available free at otexts.com/fpp3

title: Week 8: Guided Example – Regression
text:
This is the first of five guided examples. Each one walks through a complete analysis from question to communication. Today: linear regression – the workhorse of statistical modeling.










NoteBy the end of this lesson, you will be able to:




Fit simple and multiple linear regression models in R
Interpret coefficients in context
Perform residual diagnostics
Distinguish prediction from inference
Use broom for tidy model output
Complete a guided analysis start to finish








How does a car’s weight and engine size relate to its fuel efficiency?

We’ll use mtcars as our working example, then you’ll apply the same workflow to your own data.





library(tidyverse)
library(broom)

# Fit a simple linear model
model_simple <- lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars)

# Summary
summary(model_simple)

# Tidy output with broom
tidy(model_simple)
glance(model_simple)




ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  labs(title = "MPG vs. Weight",
       x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
       y = "Miles per Gallon") +
  theme_minimal()





tidy(model_simple)
# Intercept: predicted MPG when weight = 0 (extrapolation -- not meaningful)
# wt slope: for each additional 1000 lbs, MPG decreases by ~5.3







# Add more predictors
model_multi <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp + cyl, data = mtcars)

tidy(model_multi)
glance(model_multi)

# Compare models
glance(model_simple)$r.squared
glance(model_multi)$r.squared





A model is only useful if its assumptions hold. Check the residuals.

# Base R diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model_multi)

# Or use augment from broom
model_multi |>
  augment() |>
  ggplot(aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Residuals vs Fitted",
       x = "Fitted Values", y = "Residuals") +
  theme_minimal()











Plot
What it checks
Red flag




Residuals vs Fitted
Linearity, constant variance
Patterns or fan shape


Normal Q-Q
Normality of residuals
Points off the line


Scale-Location
Homoscedasticity
Upward trend


Residuals vs Leverage
Influential points
Points in Cook’s distance zone









Inference: Which predictors matter? What’s the effect of weight on MPG? (Use p-values, confidence intervals)
Prediction: What MPG will a new car get? (Use predict(), prediction intervals)


# Predict for a new car
new_car <- data.frame(wt = 3.0, hp = 150, cyl = 6)
predict(model_multi, newdata = new_car, interval = "prediction")






Question: How do car attributes affect fuel efficiency?
Data: mtcars (built-in)
EDA: Scatterplots, correlations, distributions
Model: Simple → multiple regression
Diagnostics: Check residuals
Interpret: Coefficients in context
Communicate: Write it up in Quarto







TipYour Turn



Apply this exact workflow to a dataset of your choosing. Use broom for tidy output and include diagnostic plots.









You can fit, interpret, and diagnose linear regression models
You’ve seen a complete analysis from start to finish




Week 9: Guided Example – Classification

Logistic regression and decision trees
Train/test splits and confusion matrices
Intro to tidymodels

title: Week 6: Getting Data
text:
You know how to wrangle, visualize, and present data. But where does the data come from? This week you’ll learn to acquire data from files, APIs, websites, and databases – and think critically about the ethics of doing so.










NoteBy the end of this lesson, you will be able to:




Read CSV and Excel files into R
Query a REST API with httr2
Perform light web scraping with rvest
Connect to a database from R
Consider data ethics and terms of service
Use AI to help find and acquire data










library(tidyverse)

# readr (tidyverse)
df <- read_csv("data/my_data.csv")

# Base R
df <- read.csv("data/my_data.csv")

# Peek at the data
glimpse(df)





library(readxl)

df <- read_excel("data/report.xlsx", sheet = "Sheet1")

# List available sheets
excel_sheets("data/report.xlsx")






An API is a structured way to request data from a web service.

library(httr2)

# Example: request data from a public API
resp <- request("https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast") |>
  req_url_query(
    latitude = 41.39,
    longitude = -73.96,
    daily = "temperature_2m_max",
    timezone = "America/New_York"
  ) |>
  req_perform()

# Parse the JSON response
data <- resp |> resp_body_json()







TipAPI Tips




Many APIs require an API key – read the documentation
Rate limits: don’t hammer the server
Cache responses when developing so you don’t re-fetch every time







When there’s no API, you can scrape structured data from web pages.

library(rvest)

# Read a web page
page <- read_html("https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_population")

# Extract a table
tables <- page |> html_table()
pop_table <- tables[[1]]

glimpse(pop_table)







WarningEthics & Legality




Always check the site’s robots.txt and terms of service
Don’t scrape personal data without consent
Respect rate limits – add delays between requests
If an API exists, use it instead of scraping








library(DBI)
library(RSQLite)

# Connect to a SQLite database
con <- dbConnect(RSQLite::SQLite(), "data/my_database.db")

# List tables
dbListTables(con)

# Query
df <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM cadets WHERE gpa > 3.5")

# Or use dplyr with a database connection
library(dbplyr)
cadets_db <- tbl(con, "cadets")
cadets_db |>
  filter(gpa > 3.5) |>
  collect()

# Disconnect
dbDisconnect(con)





AI can help you discover data sources:

“What public datasets exist about [your topic]?”
“How do I access the [agency name] API in R?”
“Write httr2 code to query [specific API]”
“Parse this JSON response into a tidy data frame”







TipFor Your Capstone



Start thinking about your project topic. What data would you need? Where might it come from? Is it publicly available? Does it require an API key?









You can read data from CSV, Excel, APIs, web pages, and databases
You understand data ethics and terms of service
You’ve started thinking about data sources for your capstone

Track your work with Git
Push everything to GitHub
Write reproducible reports with Quarto
Project proposals due

title: Week 4: Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
EDA is how you get to know a dataset before you model anything. This week you’ll learn a systematic workflow for exploring data, identifying patterns, and forming hypotheses.










NoteBy the end of this lesson, you will be able to:




Follow the EDA workflow: question, wrangle, visualize, communicate
Calculate and interpret summary statistics
Examine distributions, correlations, and missing data
Use AI to accelerate exploration
Formulate hypotheses from data








Ask a question – What are you trying to learn?
Wrangle – Get the data into shape
Visualize – Plot everything; look for patterns, outliers, surprises
Summarize – Compute statistics that quantify what you see
Communicate – Tell someone what you found

This is iterative. Each visualization raises new questions.





library(tidyverse)

# Quick summary
summary(mtcars)

# Targeted summaries
mtcars |>
  summarize(
    mean_mpg = mean(mpg),
    median_mpg = median(mpg),
    sd_mpg = sd(mpg),
    iqr_mpg = IQR(mpg),
    min_mpg = min(mpg),
    max_mpg = max(mpg)
  )

# By group
mtcars |>
  group_by(cyl) |>
  summarize(across(c(mpg, wt, hp), list(mean = mean, sd = sd)))






# Histogram
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = mpg)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 12, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of MPG")

# Density plot
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = mpg, fill = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(fill = "Cylinders")

# Boxplot comparison
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl), y = mpg)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightblue") +
  labs(x = "Cylinders", y = "MPG")






# Pairwise correlations
mtcars |>
  select(mpg, wt, hp, disp) |>
  cor() |>
  round(2)

# Scatterplot matrix
mtcars |>
  select(mpg, wt, hp, disp) |>
  pairs()

# Using GGally
library(GGally)
mtcars |>
  select(mpg, wt, hp, cyl) |>
  ggpairs(aes(color = factor(mtcars$cyl)))






# Check for missing values
colSums(is.na(airquality))

# Visualize missingness
library(naniar)
gg_miss_var(airquality)
vis_miss(airquality)

# Strategies
# 1. Remove rows with missing values
airquality |> drop_na()

# 2. Remove rows missing in specific columns
airquality |> drop_na(Ozone)

# 3. Impute (replace with median, mean, etc.)
airquality |>
  mutate(Ozone = replace_na(Ozone, median(Ozone, na.rm = TRUE)))





AI is particularly useful during EDA:

“What are common ways to explore this type of data?”
“Write code to check for outliers in this column”
“Suggest visualizations for comparing these two variables”
Paste glimpse(df) output and ask for an EDA starter script







TipTry This



Pick a built-in R dataset (e.g., diamonds, storms, starwars). Ask Claude to generate an EDA script. Run it, then modify it based on what you find interesting.









You have a systematic EDA workflow
You can compute summary statistics and visualize distributions
You can check correlations and handle missing data




Week 5: Interactive Apps with Shiny

Turn your EDA into an interactive Shiny app
Reactive inputs and outputs
Deploy to shinyapps.io or host on your Pi

title: Week 2: R & RStudio on the Pi
text:
This week you’ll install R and RStudio Server on your Raspberry Pi, access it through a browser, and learn the fundamentals of R programming.










NoteBy the end of this lesson, you will be able to:




Install R and RStudio Server on the Raspberry Pi
Access RStudio Server via a web browser
Work with R basics: vectors, data frames, functions, and packages
Install and load the tidyverse
Write and run your first R script







# SSH into your Pi (or use the terminal directly)
sudo apt update
sudo apt install r-base r-base-dev
Verify the installation:
R --version




RStudio Server lets you use the RStudio IDE from any browser on your network – no monitor needed on the Pi.
# Install dependencies
sudo apt install gdebi-core

# Download and install RStudio Server (ARM version for Pi)
# Check https://posit.co/download/rstudio-server/ for the latest URL
sudo gdebi rstudio-server-<version>-arm64.deb
Once installed, open a browser on your laptop and go to:
http://<your-pi-ip>:8787
Log in with your Pi username and password. You now have a full RStudio IDE running on your Pi, accessible from your laptop.






The fundamental data structure in R:

# Create vectors
x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
names <- c("Alpha", "Bravo", "Charlie")

# Vector operations
length(x)

[1] 5

sum(x)

[1] 15

mean(x)

[1] 3

x * 2

[1]  2  4  6  8 10





Tables of data – each column is a vector:

# Create a data frame
cadets <- data.frame(
  name = c("Adams", "Baker", "Clark"),
  company = c("A", "B", "C"),
  gpa = c(3.5, 3.2, 3.8)
)

cadets

   name company gpa
1 Adams       A 3.5
2 Baker       B 3.2
3 Clark       C 3.8






# Access columns
cadets$gpa

[1] 3.5 3.2 3.8

cadets[, "name"]

[1] "Adams" "Baker" "Clark"

# Access rows
cadets[1, ]

   name company gpa
1 Adams       A 3.5

# Filter
cadets[cadets$gpa > 3.3, ]

   name company gpa
1 Adams       A 3.5
3 Clark       C 3.8








# Built-in functions
sqrt(16)

[1] 4

round(3.14159, 2)

[1] 3.14

paste("Hello", "MA491")

[1] "Hello MA491"

# Write your own function
celsius_to_fahrenheit <- function(c) {
  f <- (c * 9/5) + 32
  return(f)
}

celsius_to_fahrenheit(100)

[1] 212

celsius_to_fahrenheit(0)

[1] 32






Packages extend R’s capabilities. The tidyverse is a collection of packages for data science.

# Install (only need to do this once)
install.packages("tidyverse")

# Load (do this each session)
library(tidyverse)







TipTidyverse Packages



The tidyverse includes:

dplyr – data manipulation
ggplot2 – visualization
tidyr – reshaping data
readr – reading data files
stringr – string manipulation
forcats – working with factors
purrr – functional programming
tibble – modern data frames







Create a new R script in RStudio: File > New File > R Script

# my_first_script.R
# MA491 - Week 2

library(tidyverse)

# Built-in dataset
data(mtcars)

# Quick look
glimpse(mtcars)

# Simple summary
summary(mtcars$mpg)

# Simple plot
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Car Weight vs. Fuel Efficiency",
       x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
       y = "Miles per Gallon")

Save this as my_first_script.R in your ~/ma491/scripts/ directory.

title: Week 2: R & RStudio on the Pi
text:
# SSH into your Pi (or use the terminal directly)
sudo apt update
sudo apt install r-base r-base-dev
Verify the installation:
R --version

title: Week 2: R & RStudio on the Pi
text:
RStudio Server lets you use the RStudio IDE from any browser on your network – no monitor needed on the Pi.
# Install dependencies
sudo apt install gdebi-core

# Download and install RStudio Server (ARM version for Pi)
# Check https://posit.co/download/rstudio-server/ for the latest URL
sudo gdebi rstudio-server-<version>-arm64.deb
Once installed, open a browser on your laptop and go to:
http://<your-pi-ip>:8787
Log in with your Pi username and password. You now have a full RStudio IDE running on your Pi, accessible from your laptop.

title: Week 2: R & RStudio on the Pi
text:
The fundamental data structure in R:

# Create vectors
x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
names <- c("Alpha", "Bravo", "Charlie")

# Vector operations
length(x)

[1] 5

sum(x)

[1] 15

mean(x)

[1] 3

x * 2

[1]  2  4  6  8 10





Tables of data – each column is a vector:

# Create a data frame
cadets <- data.frame(
  name = c("Adams", "Baker", "Clark"),
  company = c("A", "B", "C"),
  gpa = c(3.5, 3.2, 3.8)
)

cadets

   name company gpa
1 Adams       A 3.5
2 Baker       B 3.2
3 Clark       C 3.8






# Access columns
cadets$gpa

[1] 3.5 3.2 3.8

cadets[, "name"]

[1] "Adams" "Baker" "Clark"

# Access rows
cadets[1, ]

   name company gpa
1 Adams       A 3.5

# Filter
cadets[cadets$gpa > 3.3, ]

   name company gpa
1 Adams       A 3.5
3 Clark       C 3.8

# Built-in functions
sqrt(16)

[1] 4

round(3.14159, 2)

[1] 3.14

paste("Hello", "MA491")

[1] "Hello MA491"

# Write your own function
celsius_to_fahrenheit <- function(c) {
  f <- (c * 9/5) + 32
  return(f)
}

celsius_to_fahrenheit(100)

[1] 212

celsius_to_fahrenheit(0)

[1] 32

title: Week 2: R & RStudio on the Pi
text:
Packages extend R’s capabilities. The tidyverse is a collection of packages for data science.

# Install (only need to do this once)
install.packages("tidyverse")

# Load (do this each session)
library(tidyverse)







TipTidyverse Packages



The tidyverse includes:

dplyr – data manipulation
ggplot2 – visualization
tidyr – reshaping data
readr – reading data files
stringr – string manipulation
forcats – working with factors
purrr – functional programming
tibble – modern data frames

title: Week 2: R & RStudio on the Pi

# my_first_script.R

library(tidyverse)

# Built-in dataset
data(mtcars)

# Quick look
glimpse(mtcars)

# Simple summary
summary(mtcars$mpg)

# Simple plot
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Car Weight vs. Fuel Efficiency",
       x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
       y = "Miles per Gallon")

10 minutes, no more

Pair up and present to each other. For each presentation, give feedback on:

Clarity: Can you understand the question and results?
Visuals: Are the plots effective? Is the Shiny app intuitive?

Start with the question, not the data
Show key visuals, not code
Demo the Shiny app live
End with what you learned and what you’d do next
Practice transitions – 10 minutes goes fast
Anticipate questions

You’ve practiced your presentation and received feedback

text:
Your data is clean and you’ve explored it. Now it’s time to model and start writing up your results.










NoteGoals for Work Session 2




Apply your chosen modeling technique(s) to the data

Whatever approach you’re using, follow the workflow:

Split your data (train/test) if doing prediction
Fit the model on training data
Evaluate on test data (or use cross-validation)
Diagnose – check residuals, assumptions, edge cases
Iterate – try alternative models or features

If your project involves…

title: Week 12: Guided Example – Time Series & Forecasting
text:
Data that arrives over time has special structure: trends, seasonality, and autocorrelation. This week you’ll learn to decompose, visualize, and forecast time series data using modern R tools.










NoteBy the end of this lesson, you will be able to:




Understand time series structure (trend, seasonality, residual)
Create and work with tsibble objects
Decompose a time series into its components
Fit and evaluate forecasting models with fable
Understand ARIMA basics
Visualize and forecast a real time series







A time series can be decomposed into:

Trend: Long-term increase or decrease
Seasonality: Regular periodic patterns (weekly, monthly, yearly)
Residual: What’s left after removing trend and seasonality






library(tidyverse)
library(tsibble)
library(feasts)
library(fable)

# Example: Australian tourism data
data(tourism, package = "tsibble")
glimpse(tourism)

# Or convert your own data
my_ts <- data.frame(
  date = seq(as.Date("2020-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = 36),
  value = cumsum(rnorm(36, mean = 2))
) |>
  as_tsibble(index = date)






# Time plot
tourism |>
  filter(Region == "Melbourne", Purpose == "Holiday") |>
  autoplot(Trips) +
  labs(title = "Melbourne Holiday Tourism",
       y = "Trips (thousands)") +
  theme_minimal()

# Season plot
tourism |>
  filter(Region == "Melbourne", Purpose == "Holiday") |>
  gg_season(Trips) +
  labs(title = "Seasonal Pattern") +
  theme_minimal()

# Subseries plot
tourism |>
  filter(Region == "Melbourne", Purpose == "Holiday") |>
  gg_subseries(Trips) +
  theme_minimal()






tourism |>
  filter(Region == "Melbourne", Purpose == "Holiday") |>
  model(STL(Trips ~ season(window = "periodic"))) |>
  components() |>
  autoplot() +
  labs(title = "STL Decomposition: Melbourne Holiday Tourism") +
  theme_minimal()






# Prepare data
melb_holiday <- tourism |>
  filter(Region == "Melbourne", Purpose == "Holiday")

# Fit multiple models
fit <- melb_holiday |>
  model(
    ets = ETS(Trips),
    arima = ARIMA(Trips),
    naive = NAIVE(Trips),
    snaive = SNAIVE(Trips)
  )

# Forecast 2 years ahead
fc <- fit |> forecast(h = "2 years")

# Plot forecasts
fc |>
  autoplot(melb_holiday, level = 80) +
  labs(title = "Tourism Forecasts: Melbourne Holiday",
       y = "Trips (thousands)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_wrap(~.model)






# Accuracy on training data
accuracy(fit)

# Cross-validation for more robust evaluation
melb_holiday |>
  stretch_tsibble(.init = 12, .step = 4) |>
  model(
    ets = ETS(Trips),
    arima = ARIMA(Trips)
  ) |>
  forecast(h = 4) |>
  accuracy(melb_holiday)






Metric
What it measures




MAE
Average absolute error


RMSE
Root mean squared error (penalizes large errors)


MAPE
Mean absolute percentage error


MASE
Mean absolute scaled error (compared to naive forecast)








ARIMA (AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average) has three components:

AR(p): The value depends on its own past values
I(d): Differencing to make the series stationary
MA(q): The value depends on past forecast errors


# Auto ARIMA -- let R choose the best p, d, q
arima_fit <- melb_holiday |>
  model(ARIMA(Trips))

report(arima_fit)

# Residual diagnostics
arima_fit |> gg_tsresiduals()








You can decompose and visualize time series
You can fit and compare forecasting models
You understand ARIMA at a conceptual level

Start your individual project
Focus on data acquisition and initial EDA
Instructor check-ins begin

title: Week 10: Guided Example – Text as Data
text:
Not all data comes in neat rows and columns. Text – reports, doctrine, news articles, social media – is one of the richest data sources available. This week you’ll learn to treat text as data: clean it, count it, and extract meaning from it.










NoteBy the end of this lesson, you will be able to:




Manipulate strings with stringr
Tokenize text and compute word frequencies with tidytext
Perform sentiment analysis
Calculate and interpret TF-IDF
Apply text analysis to a real corpus








library(tidyverse)

text <- "The United States Military Academy at West Point, NY 10996"

str_to_lower(text)
str_detect(text, "West Point")
str_replace(text, "NY", "New York")
str_extract(text, "\\d{5}")          # extract zip code
str_split(text, " ")                  # split into words
str_length(text)





The tidy text principle: one token per row.

library(tidytext)

# Example: Jane Austen's novels
library(janeaustenr)

tidy_books <- austen_books() |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text)

# Word frequencies
tidy_books |>
  anti_join(stop_words) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  head(20)



Common words (“the”, “and”, “is”) that add noise. Remove them:

# View stop words
stop_words

# Remove them
tidy_books |>
  anti_join(stop_words) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 15) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(word, n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Most Common Words (excluding stop words)",
       x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()






Assign emotional valence to words using a sentiment lexicon.

# Using the Bing lexicon (positive/negative)
sentiment_counts <- tidy_books |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing")) |>
  count(book, sentiment) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = sentiment, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) |>
  mutate(net_sentiment = positive - negative)

sentiment_counts

# Sentiment over the course of a novel
austen_books() |>
  filter(book == "Pride & Prejudice") |>
  mutate(line_number = row_number(),
         chapter_group = line_number %/% 80) |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing")) |>
  count(chapter_group, sentiment) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = sentiment, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) |>
  mutate(net = positive - negative) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = chapter_group, y = net)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Sentiment Arc: Pride & Prejudice",
       x = "Narrative Time", y = "Net Sentiment") +
  theme_minimal()





Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency: identifies words that are important to a specific document relative to the whole corpus.

book_words <- austen_books() |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  count(book, word, sort = TRUE)

book_tfidf <- book_words |>
  bind_tf_idf(word, book, n)

# Top TF-IDF words per book
book_tfidf |>
  group_by(book) |>
  slice_max(tf_idf, n = 10) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder_within(word, tf_idf, book), y = tf_idf, fill = book)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~book, scales = "free") +
  scale_x_reordered() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top TF-IDF Words by Novel", x = NULL, y = "TF-IDF") +
  theme_minimal()











TipTry This



Apply text analysis to a militarily-relevant corpus:

Army doctrine publications (ADPs, FMs)
After Action Reviews (AARs)
News coverage of military operations
Congressional testimony transcripts

Questions to explore:

How has the language of Army doctrine changed over time?
What topics dominate AARs from different units or eras?
How does media sentiment toward the military shift during conflicts?










You can tokenize, count, and visualize text data
You understand sentiment analysis and TF-IDF
You’ve seen how to apply text methods to real corpora




Week 11: Guided Example – Spatial Data & Mapping

sf for spatial data
leaflet for interactive maps
Choropleth maps and spatial joins

title: MA491: Data Science Practicum
text:
AY27-1 | United States Military Academy





MA491 is a capstone practicum for non-thesis cadets in the Department of Mathematical Sciences. The course takes a “cradle to grave” approach to data science: starting with bare hardware (Raspberry Pi), building up infrastructure and tools, working through guided examples, and culminating in an individual end-to-end project.
Language: R (with command-line/Linux as infrastructure)
Final Deliverable: Individual project including a GitHub repo, Quarto report, Shiny app, and presentation







 Canvas Grades & Assignments 




 Syllabus Course Syllabus 




 R for Data Science Wickham, Cetinkaya-Rundel, Grolemund 









LTC Dusty Turner, PhD Academy Professor, Department of Mathematical Sciences

Office: Thayer Hall
Email: dusty.turner@westpoint.edu
Website: dustysturner.com

Email to coordinate Additional Instruction.














Phase
Weeks
Focus




Setup & Core Skills
1–3
Raspberry Pi, R & RStudio, data wrangling, visualization, AI tools


Building the Toolkit
4–7
EDA, Shiny, data acquisition, Git & Quarto


Guided Examples
8–12
Regression, classification, text, spatial, time series


Capstone
13–17
Individual project work sessions & final presentations







This site contains instructor notes and lesson resources for MA491. Use the sidebar to navigate through lessons.

title: MA491: Data Science Practicum
text:
MA491 is a capstone practicum for non-thesis cadets in the Department of Mathematical Sciences. The course takes a “cradle to grave” approach to data science: starting with bare hardware (Raspberry Pi), building up infrastructure and tools, working through guided examples, and culminating in an individual end-to-end project.
Language: R (with command-line/Linux as infrastructure)
Final Deliverable: Individual project including a GitHub repo, Quarto report, Shiny app, and presentation

title: Lesson 8: Counting & Independence
text:
NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 6



Sample Spaces and Events:

Sample space \(S\) = set of all possible outcomes
Event = subset of the sample space
Operations: Union (\(A \cup B\)), Intersection (\(A \cap B\)), Complement (\(A^c\))

Kolmogorov Axioms:

\(P(A) \geq 0\)
\(P(S) = 1\)
For mutually exclusive events: \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)\)

Key Rules:

Complement Rule: \(P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)\)
Addition Rule: \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)\)












NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 7



Conditional Probability: \[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}\]
Multiplication Rule: \[P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)\]
Law of Total Probability: \[P(A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)\]
Bayes’ Theorem: \[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)}\]










Count sample spaces of ordered or unordered events with or without replacement
Apply the multiplication principle to count outcomes
Distinguish between permutations and combinations
Understand how to account for independent events




Devore, Sections 2.3 and 2.5






















































































NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 8



Multiplication Principle: If an experiment has stages with \(n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k\) outcomes, the total outcomes are \(n_1 \times n_2 \times \cdots \times n_k\).
Counting Formulas:









With Replacement
Without Replacement




Ordered
\(n^k\)
\(P(n,k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!}\)


Unordered
\(\binom{n+k-1}{k}\)
\(\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}\)



Independence:

\(A\) and \(B\) are independent if \(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\)
Equivalently: \(P(A \mid B) = P(A)\)
Independent \(\neq\) Mutually Exclusive!
For independent events: \(P(A_1 \cap \cdots \cap A_n) = \prod P(A_i)\)












Recall from Lesson 6: when all outcomes are equally likely,
\[P(A) = \frac{|A|}{|S|} = \frac{\text{number of outcomes in } A}{\text{total number of outcomes}}\]
To use this formula, we need to count outcomes. For simple experiments (one die, one card), this is easy. But what about more complex experiments?
Example: How many 5-card poker hands are possible from a standard 52-card deck?
We need systematic counting techniques!













ImportantThe Multiplication Principle



If an experiment consists of two stages, where:

Stage 1 has \(n_1\) possible outcomes
Stage 2 has \(n_2\) possible outcomes

Then the total number of outcomes for the experiment is:
\[n_1 \times n_2\]
This extends to \(k\) stages:
\[n_1 \times n_2 \times \cdots \times n_k\]






My daughter’s softball team (the Warriors) has accumulated quite a collection of gear. How many unique uniform combinations can she put together?






Jerseys (4): royal blue, white, pink, and an accidental purple
Pants (3): black, white, blue
Socks (2): black, blue
Batting gloves (2): two different pairs

\[\text{Total combinations} = 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 2 = 48\]







When selecting items from a group, ask yourself:

Does order matter? (Is selecting A then B different from B then A?)
Is replacement allowed? (Can the same item be selected again?)










With Replacement
Without Replacement




Ordered
\(n^k\)
\(P(n,k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!}\)


Unordered
\(\binom{n+k-1}{k}\)
\(\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}\)










When we select \(k\) items from \(n\) options with replacement and order matters:
\[\text{Number of outcomes} = n^k\]
Example: There are 18 cadets in your section. You have 3 questions to ask during class. For each question, a random cadet is selected to answer. The same cadet can be called on more than once.

Order matters: Being called on for Question 1 is different from Question 2
Replacement: The same cadet can be called again

\[18^3 = 5,\!832 \text{ possible sequences}\]







When we select \(k\) items from \(n\) options without replacement and order matters, we get a permutation:
\[P(n,k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!} = n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \cdots \times (n-k+1)\]
Special case: arranging all \(n\) items gives \(n!\) permutations.
Why? For the first selection, we have \(n\) choices. For the second, \(n-1\) (one item is used). For the third, \(n-2\), and so on.



Same 18 cadets. Now you assign 3 different cadets to present Board Problems 1, 2, and 3 on the board.

Order matters: Who presents Problem 1 vs. Problem 2 is a different assignment
No replacement: Each cadet presents at most one problem

\[P(n, k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!} = \frac{18!}{(18-3)!} = \frac{18!}{15!} = 18 \times 17 \times 16 = 4,\!896\]
Compare to with replacement: \(18^3 = 5,\!832\) — fewer options when we can’t repeat!







When we select \(k\) items from \(n\) options without replacement and order does NOT matter, we get a combination:
\[\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!} = \frac{P(n,k)}{k!}\]
Read “\(\binom{n}{k}\)” as “\(n\) choose \(k\).”
Why divide by \(k!\)? Permutations count every ordering of the same \(k\) items as different. Since we don’t care about order, we divide out the \(k!\) rearrangements.



Same 18 cadets. You choose 3 to form a study group. There are no roles — just who’s in the group.

Order doesn’t matter: The group {Adams, Baker, Clark} is the same regardless of the order you picked them
No replacement: Each cadet is either in the group or not

\[\binom{18}{3} = \frac{18!}{3! \cdot 15!} = \frac{18 \times 17 \times 16}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 816\]
Compare to permutations: \(P(18,3) = 4,\!896\). Since we no longer care about order, we divide out the \(3! = 6\) rearrangements: \(4,\!896 / 6 = 816\) .










TipWhen Does Order Matter?



Order matters (Permutation):

Assigning ranks/positions (1st place vs. 2nd place)
Passwords, PINs, codes
Sequences of events

Order does NOT matter (Combination):

Selecting a group/team/committee
Choosing cards for a hand
Picking items from a menu (when the set matters, not the sequence)



Quick comparison: Choosing 3 from our 18 cadets:

Permutations (board problems): \(P(18,3) = 4,\!896\) — each assignment of cadets to specific problems is different
Combinations (study group): \(\binom{18}{3} = 816\) — only group membership matters
Note: \(4,\!896 / 3! = 4,\!896 / 6 = 816\) ✓








When we select \(k\) items from \(n\) options with replacement and order does NOT matter:
\[\binom{n+k-1}{k} = \frac{(n+k-1)!}{k!\,(n-1)!}\]
Why? Imagine laying out \(k\) identical stars (the items you’re choosing) in a row. You need \(n-1\) bars to divide those stars into \(n\) bins. Any arrangement of stars and bars gives a valid selection, so the count equals the number of ways to place the \(n-1\) bars among the \(k + (n-1)\) total positions: \(\binom{n+k-1}{k}\).



Same 18 cadets. You have 3 identical bonus points to award. A cadet can receive more than one point — only the total each cadet receives matters, not the order you hand them out.

Order doesn’t matter: Just the final distribution of points
Replacement: A cadet can receive multiple points

Picture 3 stars and 17 bars (one bar between each of the 18 cadets). Choose where to place the 3 stars among the \(3 + 17 = 20\) positions:
\[\binom{n+k-1}{k} = \binom{18 + 3 - 1}{3} = \binom{20}{3} = \frac{20 \times 19 \times 18}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 1,\!140\]







Many probability problems reduce to counting:
\[P(A) = \frac{\text{number of outcomes in } A}{\text{total outcomes in } S} = \frac{|A|}{|S|}\]



Your section has 18 cadets drawn from all 4 regiments. Six are from 1st Regiment. You randomly select 3 cadets for a study group. What is the probability all 3 are from 1st Regiment?
Total ways to choose 3 from 18:
\[|S| = \binom{18}{3} = \frac{18!}{3! \cdot 15!} = \frac{18 \times 17 \times 16}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 816\]
Ways to choose 3 from the 6 first-regiment cadets:
\[|A| = \binom{6}{3} = \frac{6!}{3! \cdot 3!} = \frac{6 \times 5 \times 4}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 20\]
Probability:
\[P(\text{all 1st Regiment}) = \frac{20}{816} = \frac{5}{204} \approx 0.0245\]







Two events are independent if knowing one occurred doesn’t change the probability of the other.
Events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if:
\[P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\]
Equivalently: \(P(A \mid B) = P(A)\) and \(P(B \mid A) = P(B)\)
Intuition: Learning that \(B\) happened doesn’t update your belief about \(A\).




Roll a fair die. Let:

\(A\) = “roll an even number” = \(\{2, 4, 6\}\)
\(B\) = “roll a number \(\leq 3\)” = \(\{1, 2, 3\}\)

Check independence:

\(P(A) = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}\)
\(P(B) = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}\)
\(P(A \cap B) = P(\{2\}) = \frac{1}{6}\)
\(P(A) \cdot P(B) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}\)

Since \(\frac{1}{6} \neq \frac{1}{4}\), events \(A\) and \(B\) are NOT independent.
Also verify: \(P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} = \frac{1/6}{1/2} = \frac{1}{3} \neq \frac{1}{2} = P(A)\)
Knowing the roll is \(\leq 3\) changes the probability of an even number.




Roll a fair die. Let:

\(A\) = “roll an even number” = \(\{2, 4, 6\}\)
\(C\) = “roll a number divisible by 3” = \(\{3, 6\}\)

Check independence:

\(P(A) = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}\)
\(P(C) = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}\)
\(P(A \cap C) = P(\{6\}) = \frac{1}{6}\)
\(P(A) \cdot P(C) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{1}{6}\)

Since \(\frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{6}\), events \(A\) and \(C\) ARE independent!
Knowing the roll is divisible by 3 doesn’t change the probability of it being even — it’s still \(\frac{1}{2}\). Verify: \(P(A \mid C) = \frac{P(A \cap C)}{P(C)} = \frac{1/6}{1/3} = \frac{1}{2} = P(A)\) ✓




Independence arises naturally when experiments are physically unrelated:

Flipping a coin, then rolling a die
Drawing from one deck, then drawing from a different deck
Two different cadets’ test scores (assuming no cheating!)
Successive tosses of a fair coin







WarningIndependence vs. Mutually Exclusive



These are DIFFERENT concepts!

Mutually exclusive: \(A \cap B = \emptyset\) — they CANNOT both happen
Independent: \(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\) — one doesn’t affect the other

If \(A\) and \(B\) are mutually exclusive with \(P(A) > 0\) and \(P(B) > 0\), then they are NOT independent (knowing \(A\) occurred means \(B\) definitely didn’t!).








A battalion commander must plan an operation by choosing:

1 of 4 companies to lead the assault
1 of 3 routes to the objective
1 of 2 times for the attack (dawn or dusk)







NoteQuestion





How many different operation plans are possible?









TipAnswer





Each choice is a separate stage with a different number of options: \[4 \times 3 \times 2 = 24\]







A cadet’s locker combination is a sequence of 4 digits from 0 to 9 (digits may repeat).






NoteQuestions






Does order matter?
Is this with or without replacement?
How many possible combinations are there?
What is the probability of guessing correctly on the first try?










TipAnswers






Yes — the sequence 1-2-3-4 is different from 4-3-2-1.
With replacement — digits may repeat.

Ordered, with replacement (\(n = 10\), \(k = 4\)):

\[n^k = 10^4 = 10,\!000\]
\[P(\text{correct on first try}) = \frac{1}{10,\!000} = 0.0001\]








From a squad of 12 cadets, the platoon leader must assign three distinct roles: team leader, assistant team leader, and radio operator.






NoteQuestions






Does order matter?
Is this with or without replacement?
How many ways can these 3 positions be filled?
What is the probability that a specific cadet (CDT Jones) is assigned team leader?










TipAnswers






Yes — being assigned team leader is different from being assigned radio operator.
Without replacement — each cadet fills at most one role.

Ordered, without replacement (\(n = 12\), \(k = 3\)):

\[P(n,k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!} = \frac{12!}{9!} = 12 \times 11 \times 10 = 1,\!320\]
If CDT Jones is team leader, the remaining 2 roles are filled from the other 11: \[P(11, 2) = 11 \times 10 = 110\] \[P(\text{Jones is team leader}) = \frac{110}{1,\!320} = \frac{1}{12}\]

This makes sense — each of the 12 cadets is equally likely to be team leader.







A cadet buys 4 drinks from a vending machine that stocks 3 flavors (Gatorade, Celsius, water). Repeats are allowed.






NoteQuestions






Does order matter?
Is this with or without replacement?
How many different selections are possible?










TipAnswers






No — buying Gatorade then Celsius is the same selection as Celsius then Gatorade. Only the total of each flavor matters.
With replacement — you can buy the same flavor more than once.

Unordered, with replacement (\(n = 3\) types, \(k = 4\) drinks):

\[\binom{n+k-1}{k} = \binom{3+4-1}{4} = \binom{6}{4} = \frac{6!}{4! \cdot 2!} = \frac{6 \times 5}{2 \times 1} = 15\]

Stars and bars: place 4 stars among 3 bins separated by 2 bars → choose 4 positions from 6 total.







At a marksmanship qualification, data from 200 cadets shows:

160 qualified Expert on the M4 rifle
120 qualified Expert on the M9 pistol
96 qualified Expert on both







NoteQuestions






What is \(P(\text{M4 Expert})\)?
What is \(P(\text{M9 Expert})\)?
What is \(P(\text{M4 Expert AND M9 Expert})\)?
What is \(P(\text{M4 Expert} \mid \text{M9 Expert})\)?
Are M4 and M9 qualifications independent? Verify using the definition.










TipAnswers






\[P(\text{M4}) = \frac{160}{200} = 0.80\]
\[P(\text{M9}) = \frac{120}{200} = 0.60\]
\[P(\text{M4} \cap \text{M9}) = \frac{96}{200} = 0.48\]
\[P(\text{M4} \mid \text{M9}) = \frac{P(\text{M4} \cap \text{M9})}{P(\text{M9})} = \frac{0.48}{0.60} = 0.80\]
Yes, they are independent. Two ways to verify:


\(P(\text{M4} \mid \text{M9}) = 0.80 = P(\text{M4})\) — knowing a cadet qualified Expert on the M9 doesn’t change the probability they qualified Expert on the M4. ✓
\(P(\text{M4}) \cdot P(\text{M9}) = 0.80 \times 0.60 = 0.48 = P(\text{M4} \cap \text{M9})\) ✓












The multiplication principle for counting outcomes
Permutations (ordered) vs. Combinations (unordered)
With vs. without replacement
Independence: \(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\)
Multiplication rule for independent events

Any questions?




Lesson 9: Discrete Random Variables

Probability mass functions (PMFs)
Cumulative distribution functions (CDFs)
Expected value and variance






WebAssign 2.3, 2.5 - Due before Lesson 9
Exploratory Data Analysis - Due Lesson 9
WPR I - Lesson 16

title: Lesson 8: Counting & Independence
text:
NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 6



Sample Spaces and Events:

Sample space \(S\) = set of all possible outcomes
Event = subset of the sample space
Operations: Union (\(A \cup B\)), Intersection (\(A \cap B\)), Complement (\(A^c\))

Kolmogorov Axioms:

\(P(A) \geq 0\)
\(P(S) = 1\)
For mutually exclusive events: \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)\)

Key Rules:

Complement Rule: \(P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)\)
Addition Rule: \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)\)












NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 7



Conditional Probability: \[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}\]
Multiplication Rule: \[P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)\]
Law of Total Probability: \[P(A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)\]
Bayes’ Theorem: \[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)}\]

text:
NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 8



Multiplication Principle: If an experiment has stages with \(n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k\) outcomes, the total outcomes are \(n_1 \times n_2 \times \cdots \times n_k\).
Counting Formulas:









With Replacement
Without Replacement




Ordered
\(n^k\)
\(P(n,k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!}\)


Unordered
\(\binom{n+k-1}{k}\)
\(\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}\)



Independence:

\(A\) and \(B\) are independent if \(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\)
Equivalently: \(P(A \mid B) = P(A)\)
Independent \(\neq\) Mutually Exclusive!
For independent events: \(P(A_1 \cap \cdots \cap A_n) = \prod P(A_i)\)

text:
Recall from Lesson 6: when all outcomes are equally likely,
\[P(A) = \frac{|A|}{|S|} = \frac{\text{number of outcomes in } A}{\text{total number of outcomes}}\]
To use this formula, we need to count outcomes. For simple experiments (one die, one card), this is easy. But what about more complex experiments?
Example: How many 5-card poker hands are possible from a standard 52-card deck?
We need systematic counting techniques!

If an experiment consists of two stages, where:

Stage 1 has \(n_1\) possible outcomes
Stage 2 has \(n_2\) possible outcomes

Then the total number of outcomes for the experiment is:
\[n_1 \times n_2\]
This extends to \(k\) stages:
\[n_1 \times n_2 \times \cdots \times n_k\]






My daughter’s softball team (the Warriors) has accumulated quite a collection of gear. How many unique uniform combinations can she put together?






Jerseys (4): royal blue, white, pink, and an accidental purple
Pants (3): black, white, blue
Socks (2): black, blue
Batting gloves (2): two different pairs

\[\text{Total combinations} = 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 2 = 48\]

title: Lesson 8: Counting & Independence
text:
When selecting items from a group, ask yourself:

Does order matter? (Is selecting A then B different from B then A?)
Is replacement allowed? (Can the same item be selected again?)










With Replacement
Without Replacement




Ordered
\(n^k\)
\(P(n,k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!}\)


Unordered
\(\binom{n+k-1}{k}\)
\(\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}\)

title: Lesson 8: Counting & Independence
text:
When we select \(k\) items from \(n\) options with replacement and order matters:
\[\text{Number of outcomes} = n^k\]
Example: There are 18 cadets in your section. You have 3 questions to ask during class. For each question, a random cadet is selected to answer. The same cadet can be called on more than once.

Order matters: Being called on for Question 1 is different from Question 2
Replacement: The same cadet can be called again

\[18^3 = 5,\!832 \text{ possible sequences}\]

title: Lesson 8: Counting & Independence
text:
When we select \(k\) items from \(n\) options without replacement and order matters, we get a permutation:
\[P(n,k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!} = n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \cdots \times (n-k+1)\]
Special case: arranging all \(n\) items gives \(n!\) permutations.
Why? For the first selection, we have \(n\) choices. For the second, \(n-1\) (one item is used). For the third, \(n-2\), and so on.



Same 18 cadets. Now you assign 3 different cadets to present Board Problems 1, 2, and 3 on the board.

Order matters: Who presents Problem 1 vs. Problem 2 is a different assignment
No replacement: Each cadet presents at most one problem

\[P(n, k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!} = \frac{18!}{(18-3)!} = \frac{18!}{15!} = 18 \times 17 \times 16 = 4,\!896\]
Compare to with replacement: \(18^3 = 5,\!832\) — fewer options when we can’t repeat!

title: Lesson 8: Counting & Independence
text:
When we select \(k\) items from \(n\) options without replacement and order does NOT matter, we get a combination:
\[\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!} = \frac{P(n,k)}{k!}\]
Read “\(\binom{n}{k}\)” as “\(n\) choose \(k\).”
Why divide by \(k!\)? Permutations count every ordering of the same \(k\) items as different. Since we don’t care about order, we divide out the \(k!\) rearrangements.



Same 18 cadets. You choose 3 to form a study group. There are no roles — just who’s in the group.

Order doesn’t matter: The group {Adams, Baker, Clark} is the same regardless of the order you picked them
No replacement: Each cadet is either in the group or not

\[\binom{18}{3} = \frac{18!}{3! \cdot 15!} = \frac{18 \times 17 \times 16}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 816\]
Compare to permutations: \(P(18,3) = 4,\!896\). Since we no longer care about order, we divide out the \(3! = 6\) rearrangements: \(4,\!896 / 6 = 816\) .










TipWhen Does Order Matter?



Order matters (Permutation):

Assigning ranks/positions (1st place vs. 2nd place)
Passwords, PINs, codes
Sequences of events

Order does NOT matter (Combination):

Selecting a group/team/committee
Choosing cards for a hand
Picking items from a menu (when the set matters, not the sequence)



Quick comparison: Choosing 3 from our 18 cadets:

Permutations (board problems): \(P(18,3) = 4,\!896\) — each assignment of cadets to specific problems is different
Combinations (study group): \(\binom{18}{3} = 816\) — only group membership matters
Note: \(4,\!896 / 3! = 4,\!896 / 6 = 816\) ✓

title: Lesson 8: Counting & Independence
text:
When we select \(k\) items from \(n\) options with replacement and order does NOT matter:
\[\binom{n+k-1}{k} = \frac{(n+k-1)!}{k!\,(n-1)!}\]
Why? Imagine laying out \(k\) identical stars (the items you’re choosing) in a row. You need \(n-1\) bars to divide those stars into \(n\) bins. Any arrangement of stars and bars gives a valid selection, so the count equals the number of ways to place the \(n-1\) bars among the \(k + (n-1)\) total positions: \(\binom{n+k-1}{k}\).



Same 18 cadets. You have 3 identical bonus points to award. A cadet can receive more than one point — only the total each cadet receives matters, not the order you hand them out.

Order doesn’t matter: Just the final distribution of points
Replacement: A cadet can receive multiple points

Picture 3 stars and 17 bars (one bar between each of the 18 cadets). Choose where to place the 3 stars among the \(3 + 17 = 20\) positions:
\[\binom{n+k-1}{k} = \binom{18 + 3 - 1}{3} = \binom{20}{3} = \frac{20 \times 19 \times 18}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 1,\!140\]

title: Lesson 8: Counting & Independence
text:
Many probability problems reduce to counting:
\[P(A) = \frac{\text{number of outcomes in } A}{\text{total outcomes in } S} = \frac{|A|}{|S|}\]



Your section has 18 cadets drawn from all 4 regiments. Six are from 1st Regiment. You randomly select 3 cadets for a study group. What is the probability all 3 are from 1st Regiment?
Total ways to choose 3 from 18:
\[|S| = \binom{18}{3} = \frac{18!}{3! \cdot 15!} = \frac{18 \times 17 \times 16}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 816\]
Ways to choose 3 from the 6 first-regiment cadets:
\[|A| = \binom{6}{3} = \frac{6!}{3! \cdot 3!} = \frac{6 \times 5 \times 4}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 20\]
Probability:
\[P(\text{all 1st Regiment}) = \frac{20}{816} = \frac{5}{204} \approx 0.0245\]

title: Lesson 8: Counting & Independence
text:
Two events are independent if knowing one occurred doesn’t change the probability of the other.
Events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if:
\[P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\]
Equivalently: \(P(A \mid B) = P(A)\) and \(P(B \mid A) = P(B)\)
Intuition: Learning that \(B\) happened doesn’t update your belief about \(A\).




Roll a fair die. Let:

\(A\) = “roll an even number” = \(\{2, 4, 6\}\)
\(B\) = “roll a number \(\leq 3\)” = \(\{1, 2, 3\}\)

Check independence:

\(P(A) = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}\)
\(P(B) = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}\)
\(P(A \cap B) = P(\{2\}) = \frac{1}{6}\)
\(P(A) \cdot P(B) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}\)

Since \(\frac{1}{6} \neq \frac{1}{4}\), events \(A\) and \(B\) are NOT independent.
Also verify: \(P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} = \frac{1/6}{1/2} = \frac{1}{3} \neq \frac{1}{2} = P(A)\)
Knowing the roll is \(\leq 3\) changes the probability of an even number.




Roll a fair die. Let:

\(A\) = “roll an even number” = \(\{2, 4, 6\}\)
\(C\) = “roll a number divisible by 3” = \(\{3, 6\}\)

Check independence:

\(P(A) = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}\)
\(P(C) = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}\)
\(P(A \cap C) = P(\{6\}) = \frac{1}{6}\)
\(P(A) \cdot P(C) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{1}{6}\)

Since \(\frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{6}\), events \(A\) and \(C\) ARE independent!
Knowing the roll is divisible by 3 doesn’t change the probability of it being even — it’s still \(\frac{1}{2}\). Verify: \(P(A \mid C) = \frac{P(A \cap C)}{P(C)} = \frac{1/6}{1/3} = \frac{1}{2} = P(A)\) ✓




Independence arises naturally when experiments are physically unrelated:

Flipping a coin, then rolling a die
Drawing from one deck, then drawing from a different deck
Two different cadets’ test scores (assuming no cheating!)
Successive tosses of a fair coin







WarningIndependence vs. Mutually Exclusive



These are DIFFERENT concepts!

Mutually exclusive: \(A \cap B = \emptyset\) — they CANNOT both happen
Independent: \(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\) — one doesn’t affect the other

If \(A\) and \(B\) are mutually exclusive with \(P(A) > 0\) and \(P(B) > 0\), then they are NOT independent (knowing \(A\) occurred means \(B\) definitely didn’t!).

title: Lesson 8: Counting & Independence
text:
A battalion commander must plan an operation by choosing:

1 of 4 companies to lead the assault
1 of 3 routes to the objective
1 of 2 times for the attack (dawn or dusk)







NoteQuestion





How many different operation plans are possible?









TipAnswer





Each choice is a separate stage with a different number of options: \[4 \times 3 \times 2 = 24\]







A cadet’s locker combination is a sequence of 4 digits from 0 to 9 (digits may repeat).






NoteQuestions






Does order matter?
Is this with or without replacement?
How many possible combinations are there?
What is the probability of guessing correctly on the first try?










TipAnswers






Yes — the sequence 1-2-3-4 is different from 4-3-2-1.
With replacement — digits may repeat.

Ordered, with replacement (\(n = 10\), \(k = 4\)):

\[n^k = 10^4 = 10,\!000\]
\[P(\text{correct on first try}) = \frac{1}{10,\!000} = 0.0001\]








From a squad of 12 cadets, the platoon leader must assign three distinct roles: team leader, assistant team leader, and radio operator.






NoteQuestions






Does order matter?
Is this with or without replacement?
How many ways can these 3 positions be filled?
What is the probability that a specific cadet (CDT Jones) is assigned team leader?










TipAnswers






Yes — being assigned team leader is different from being assigned radio operator.
Without replacement — each cadet fills at most one role.

Ordered, without replacement (\(n = 12\), \(k = 3\)):

\[P(n,k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!} = \frac{12!}{9!} = 12 \times 11 \times 10 = 1,\!320\]
If CDT Jones is team leader, the remaining 2 roles are filled from the other 11: \[P(11, 2) = 11 \times 10 = 110\] \[P(\text{Jones is team leader}) = \frac{110}{1,\!320} = \frac{1}{12}\]

This makes sense — each of the 12 cadets is equally likely to be team leader.







A cadet buys 4 drinks from a vending machine that stocks 3 flavors (Gatorade, Celsius, water). Repeats are allowed.






NoteQuestions






Does order matter?
Is this with or without replacement?
How many different selections are possible?










TipAnswers






No — buying Gatorade then Celsius is the same selection as Celsius then Gatorade. Only the total of each flavor matters.
With replacement — you can buy the same flavor more than once.

Unordered, with replacement (\(n = 3\) types, \(k = 4\) drinks):

\[\binom{n+k-1}{k} = \binom{3+4-1}{4} = \binom{6}{4} = \frac{6!}{4! \cdot 2!} = \frac{6 \times 5}{2 \times 1} = 15\]

Stars and bars: place 4 stars among 3 bins separated by 2 bars → choose 4 positions from 6 total.







At a marksmanship qualification, data from 200 cadets shows:

160 qualified Expert on the M4 rifle
120 qualified Expert on the M9 pistol
96 qualified Expert on both







NoteQuestions






What is \(P(\text{M4 Expert})\)?
What is \(P(\text{M9 Expert})\)?
What is \(P(\text{M4 Expert AND M9 Expert})\)?
What is \(P(\text{M4 Expert} \mid \text{M9 Expert})\)?
Are M4 and M9 qualifications independent? Verify using the definition.










TipAnswers






\[P(\text{M4}) = \frac{160}{200} = 0.80\]
\[P(\text{M9}) = \frac{120}{200} = 0.60\]
\[P(\text{M4} \cap \text{M9}) = \frac{96}{200} = 0.48\]
\[P(\text{M4} \mid \text{M9}) = \frac{P(\text{M4} \cap \text{M9})}{P(\text{M9})} = \frac{0.48}{0.60} = 0.80\]
Yes, they are independent. Two ways to verify:


\(P(\text{M4} \mid \text{M9}) = 0.80 = P(\text{M4})\) — knowing a cadet qualified Expert on the M9 doesn’t change the probability they qualified Expert on the M4. ✓
\(P(\text{M4}) \cdot P(\text{M9}) = 0.80 \times 0.60 = 0.48 = P(\text{M4} \cap \text{M9})\) ✓

text:
The multiplication principle for counting outcomes
Permutations (ordered) vs. Combinations (unordered)
With vs. without replacement
Independence: \(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\)
Multiplication rule for independent events

title: Lesson 6: Probability Basics
text:
ImportantVantage
