Calculate the median salary
Which measure would you report as the “typical” salary? Why?
Find Q1 and Q3










TipAnswers






Mean = \(\frac{45 + 48 + ... + 250}{10} = \frac{795}{10} = 79.5\) thousand ($79,500)
Ordered data (already sorted), n = 10 (even) Middle values are positions 5 and 6: 58 and 62 Median = \(\frac{58 + 62}{2} = 60\) thousand ($60,000)
The median ($60,000) is more representative. The CEO’s salary of $250,000 is an outlier that pulls the mean up to $79,500, which is higher than 80% of the employees actually earn.
Lower half: 45, 48, 52, 55, 58 → Q1 = 52 thousand Upper half: 62, 68, 72, 85, 250 → Q3 = 72 thousand








Two sections took the same quiz. Here are their scores:
Section A: 72, 75, 78, 80, 82, 85, 88 Section B: 60, 75, 78, 80, 82, 85, 100






NoteQuestions






Calculate the mean and median for each section
Which section performed better on average?
Why do the means and medians tell different stories for Section B?










TipAnswers






Section A:


Mean = 560/7 = 80
Median = 80

Section B: - Mean = 560/7 = 80 - Median = 80

Both sections have the same mean (80) and same median (80)!
This is a trick question - the means and medians are the same for both sections. However, Section B has more variability (spread from 60 to 100) compared to Section A (spread from 72 to 88). This illustrates why we need measures of spread (next lesson!) in addition to measures of center.

title: Lesson 3: Measures of Location
text:
Mean (\(\bar{x}\)): Arithmetic average; uses all data but sensitive to outliers
Median (\(\tilde{x}\)): Middle value; resistant to outliers
Mode: Most frequent value; useful for categorical data
Percentiles: Values that divide data into parts (e.g., 25th, 50th, 75th)
Quartiles: Q1, Q2 (median), Q3 divide data into four parts
Five-number summary: Min, Q1, Median, Q3, Max







ImportantRemember




For symmetric distributions: Mean ≈ Median
For skewed distributions: Median is usually preferred
Always consider outliers when choosing which measure to report

When to use each measure
Percentiles, quartiles, and the five-number summary
Boxplots as visual summaries

title: Lesson 7: Conditional Probability
text:
NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 6



Sample Spaces and Events:

Sample space \(S\) = set of all possible outcomes
Event = subset of the sample space
Operations: Union (\(A \cup B\)), Intersection (\(A \cap B\)), Complement (\(A^c\))

Kolmogorov Axioms:

\(P(A) \geq 0\)
\(P(S) = 1\)
For mutually exclusive events: \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)\)

Key Rules:

Complement Rule: \(P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)\)
Addition Rule: \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)\)











Understand and compute conditional probability \(P(A|B)\)
Apply the multiplication rule to find \(P(A \cap B)\)
Use the Law of Total Probability to find \(P(A)\)
Apply Bayes’ Theorem to “reverse” conditional probabilities




Devore, Section 2.4


















































Math vs Garrison






NotePreviously 7-2






8-2
















Let’s build a table using our class!
Round 1: Stand up if you are a Corps Squad athlete. (Count and sit)
Round 2: Stand up if you are taller than 5’9”. (Count and sit)
Round 3: Stand up if you are both (athlete AND tall). (Count and sit)




Athlete
Not Athlete
Total




Tall (>5’9”)





Short (≤5’9”)





Total










Using our data, answer these:

Of the athletes, what fraction are tall?

\[\frac{\text{Tall AND Athlete}}{\text{Total Athletes}} = \text{?}\]

Of the tall people, what fraction are athletes?

\[\frac{\text{Tall AND Athlete}}{\text{Total Tall}} = \text{?}\]






WarningKey Observation



These are different questions with different answers!
Same numerator, different denominators.


How do we write this mathematically? That’s what today’s lesson is about.







Sometimes we have partial information about an outcome. How does knowing that one event occurred change the probability of another event?
Example: You draw a card from a deck.

\(P(\text{King}) = \frac{4}{52} = \frac{1}{13}\)

But what if I tell you the card is a face card? Now:

\(P(\text{King} \mid \text{face card}) = \frac{4}{12} = \frac{1}{3}\)

The probability changed because we conditioned on new information!










ImportantConditional Probability Formula



The conditional probability of \(A\) given \(B\) is:
\[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad \text{provided } P(B) > 0\]
Read “\(P(A \mid B)\)” as “the probability of \(A\) given \(B\).”


Intuition: We’re restricting our attention to only the outcomes where \(B\) occurred, then asking what fraction of those also have \(A\).












Let’s apply the formula to our earlier example:

\(A\) = card is a King
\(B\) = card is a face card

Find each piece:

\(P(A \cap B) = P(\text{King AND face card}) = \frac{4}{52}\) (all Kings are face cards)
\(P(B) = P(\text{face card}) = \frac{12}{52}\)

Apply the formula:
\[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} = \frac{4/52}{12/52} = \frac{4}{12} = \frac{1}{3}\]
This matches our intuition: of the 12 face cards, 4 are Kings.























Rearranging the conditional probability formula:
\[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \implies P(A \cap B) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)\]






ImportantMultiplication Rule



\[P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)\]
The probability of \(A\) and \(B\) equals the probability of one event times the conditional probability of the other.











Reading the diagram:

First, find \(P(A)\) — the blue circle
Then, \(P(B \mid A)\) asks: “of those in A, what fraction are also in B?”
Multiplying gives \(P(A \cap B)\) — the purple intersection





Draw one card from a deck. What is the probability it is red AND a face card?
Let:

\(A\) = card is red
\(B\) = card is a face card

Using the multiplication rule:
\[P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)\]

\(P(A) = \frac{26}{52} = \frac{1}{2}\) (26 red cards)
\(P(B \mid A) = \frac{6}{26}\) (of the 26 red cards, 6 are face cards: J, Q, K of hearts and diamonds)

\[P(\text{red AND face card}) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6}{26} = \frac{6}{52} = \frac{3}{26} \approx 0.115\]
Check: There are 6 red face cards out of 52 total cards, so \(P = \frac{6}{52}\) ✓







Sometimes we want to find \(P(A)\), but it’s easier to compute \(P(A)\) by breaking it into cases based on another event \(B\).






ImportantLaw of Total Probability



If \(B\) and \(B^c\) partition the sample space (one must occur), then:
\[P(A) = P(A \cap B) + P(A \cap B^c)\]
Using the multiplication rule:
\[P(A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)\]















You have two boxes of cards:

Box 1: 10 cards — 4 are face cards, 6 are not
Box 2: 10 cards — 2 are face cards, 8 are not

You pick Box 1 with probability 0.70 and Box 2 with probability 0.30, then draw a card. What is P(face card)?
Let \(A\) = draw a face card, \(B\) = chose Box 1 (so \(B^c\) = chose Box 2)

\(P(B) = 0.70\), \(P(B^c) = 0.30\)
\(P(A \mid B) = \frac{4}{10} = 0.40\), \(P(A \mid B^c) = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20\)

\[P(A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)\] \[= 0.70 \times 0.40 + 0.30 \times 0.20\] \[= 0.28 + 0.06 = 0.34\]
34% of draws will be face cards.




For any partition \(B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\) of the sample space:
\[P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)\]







We often know \(P(A \mid B)\) but want \(P(B \mid A)\). Bayes’ Theorem lets us “flip” the conditioning.






ImportantBayes’ Theorem



\[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(A)}\]
Or using the Law of Total Probability in the denominator:
\[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)}\]






Using our two boxes example: if you drew a face card, what’s the probability it came from Box 1?
We have \(P(A \mid B)\) (probability of face card given Box 1), but we want \(P(B \mid A)\) (probability it was Box 1 given we drew a face card).
We know (using \(A\) = face card, \(B\) = Box 1):
- \(P(B) = 0.70\), \(P(B^c) = 0.30\)
- \(P(A \mid B) = 0.40\), \(P(A \mid B^c) = 0.20\)
- \(P(A) = 0.34\) (calculated earlier using Law of Total Probability)
Find \(P(B \mid A)\):
\[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)}\]
\[= \frac{0.70 \times 0.40}{0.70 \times 0.40 + 0.30 \times 0.20} = \frac{0.28}{0.28 + 0.06} = \frac{0.28}{0.34} \approx 0.824\]
About 82.4% of face cards came from Box 1. This is higher than 70% because Box 1 also has a higher proportion of face cards.






TipKey Insight



Bayes’ Theorem lets us “reverse” the conditional probability. We started knowing \(P(\text{face card} \mid \text{Box 1})\) and found \(P(\text{Box 1} \mid \text{face card})\).









A survey of 200 soldiers found:

140 have completed Airborne school
80 have completed Air Assault school
50 have completed both

A soldier is selected at random.






NoteQuestions






What is \(P(\text{Airborne})\)?
What is \(P(\text{Air Assault} \mid \text{Airborne})\)?
What is \(P(\text{Airborne} \mid \text{Air Assault})\)?
What is \(P(\text{Airborne AND Air Assault})\)?










TipAnswers






\(P(\text{Airborne}) = \frac{140}{200} = 0.70\)
\(P(\text{Air Assault} \mid \text{Airborne}) = \frac{P(\text{Both})}{P(\text{Airborne})} = \frac{50/200}{140/200} = \frac{50}{140} = \frac{5}{14} \approx 0.357\)
\(P(\text{Airborne} \mid \text{Air Assault}) = \frac{P(\text{Both})}{P(\text{Air Assault})} = \frac{50/200}{80/200} = \frac{50}{80} = \frac{5}{8} = 0.625\)
\(P(\text{Airborne AND Air Assault}) = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25\)








In a class of 30 cadets:

18 are in Engineering
12 take Statistics
6 are in Engineering AND take Statistics







NoteQuestions






What is \(P(\text{Statistics} \mid \text{Engineering})\)?
What is \(P(\text{Engineering} \mid \text{Statistics})\)?
What is \(P(\text{Engineering AND Statistics})\)?










TipAnswers





Let \(E\) = Engineering, \(S\) = Statistics

\[P(S \mid E) = \frac{P(S \cap E)}{P(E)} = \frac{6/30}{18/30} = \frac{6}{18} = \frac{1}{3}\]
\[P(E \mid S) = \frac{P(E \cap S)}{P(S)} = \frac{6/30}{12/30} = \frac{6}{12} = \frac{1}{2}\]
\[P(E \cap S) = \frac{6}{30} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20\]








A disease affects 2% of the population. A diagnostic test has:

Sensitivity: \(P(\text{positive} \mid \text{disease}) = 0.95\)
False positive rate: \(P(\text{positive} \mid \text{no disease}) = 0.08\)







NoteQuestions






What is \(P(\text{disease AND positive test})\)?
What is \(P(\text{no disease AND positive test})\)?
What is the overall probability of testing positive?










TipAnswers






Using the multiplication rule: \[P(D \cap +) = P(D) \cdot P(+ \mid D) = 0.02 \times 0.95 = 0.019\]
Using the multiplication rule: \[P(D^c \cap +) = P(D^c) \cdot P(+ \mid D^c) = 0.98 \times 0.08 = 0.0784\]
A positive test can happen with or without disease: \[P(+) = P(D \cap +) + P(D^c \cap +) = 0.019 + 0.0784 = 0.0974\]

About 9.74% of people will test positive.







At a checkpoint, 5% of vehicles are selected for inspection. Of the vehicles inspected, 2% are found to have violations. Of the vehicles not inspected, 0.1% have violations that go undetected.






NoteQuestions






What is the probability a randomly selected vehicle is inspected AND has a violation?
What is the probability a vehicle has a violation (detected or not)?










TipAnswers





Let \(I\) = inspected, \(V\) = has violation

Using the multiplication rule: \[P(I \cap V) = P(I) \cdot P(V \mid I) = 0.05 \times 0.02 = 0.001\]
Using the law of total probability (a vehicle either is or isn’t inspected): \[P(V) = P(I \cap V) + P(I^c \cap V)\] \[= P(I) \cdot P(V \mid I) + P(I^c) \cdot P(V \mid I^c)\] \[= 0.05 \times 0.02 + 0.95 \times 0.001\] \[= 0.001 + 0.00095 = 0.00195\]

About 0.195% of vehicles have violations.







A lock has a 4-digit code where each digit is 0-9. Assume all codes are equally likely.






NoteQuestions






How many possible codes are there?
If you guess randomly, what’s the probability of guessing correctly on the first try?
If you can try 3 times, what’s the probability of guessing correctly at least once?










TipAnswers






Each digit has 10 choices: \[10 \times 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 10^4 = 10,000 \text{ codes}\]
\[P(\text{correct on first try}) = \frac{1}{10000}\]
Using the complement (assuming you don’t repeat guesses): \[P(\text{at least one correct in 3 tries}) = 1 - P(\text{all 3 wrong})\]

If guessing without replacement from the 10,000 codes: \[P(\text{all wrong}) = \frac{9999}{10000} \cdot \frac{9998}{9999} \cdot \frac{9997}{9998} = \frac{9997}{10000}\]
\[P(\text{at least one correct}) = 1 - \frac{9997}{10000} = \frac{3}{10000} = 0.0003\]







You go on a date with Taylor Swift. Based on historical data:

30% of her dates go well
50% of her dates are meh
20% of her dates go badly

The probability Taylor writes a song about the date depends on how it went:

\(P(\text{song} \mid \text{well}) = 0.10\) (happy, less drama)
\(P(\text{song} \mid \text{meh}) = 0.30\) (some material)
\(P(\text{song} \mid \text{badly}) = 0.90\) (maximum inspiration)







NoteQuestions






What is the probability Taylor writes a song about your date?
If Taylor wrote a song about your date, what’s the probability it went badly?










TipAnswers





Let \(S\) = song written, \(W\) = went well, \(M\) = meh, \(B\) = went badly
a) Use Law of Total Probability:
\[P(S) = P(W) \cdot P(S \mid W) + P(M) \cdot P(S \mid M) + P(B) \cdot P(S \mid B)\] \[= 0.30 \times 0.10 + 0.50 \times 0.30 + 0.20 \times 0.90\] \[= 0.03 + 0.15 + 0.18 = 0.36\]
There’s a 36% chance she writes a song about your date.
b) Use Bayes’ Theorem:
\[P(B \mid S) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(S \mid B)}{P(S)} = \frac{0.20 \times 0.90}{0.36} = \frac{0.18}{0.36} = 0.50\]
If Taylor wrote a song about you, there’s a 50% chance the date went badly!
Even though only 20% of dates go badly, bad dates are so much more likely to become songs that they account for half of all songs written.
















ImportantCore Concepts



Conditional Probability: \[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}\]
Multiplication Rule: \[P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)\]
Law of Total Probability: \[P(A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)\]
Bayes’ Theorem: \[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(A)}\]










Conditional probability: \(P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}\)
The multiplication rule for \(P(A \cap B)\)
Law of Total Probability
Bayes’ Theorem: flipping conditional probabilities

Any questions?

Counting techniques (permutations, combinations)
Independence of events
Multiplication rule for independent events

Kolmogorov Axioms:

\(P(A) \geq 0\)

For mutually exclusive events: \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)\)

Key Rules:

Of the athletes, what fraction are tall?

\[\frac{\text{Tall AND Athlete}}{\text{Total Athletes}} = \text{?}\]

text:
Sometimes we have partial information about an outcome. How does knowing that one event occurred change the probability of another event?
Example: You draw a card from a deck.

\(P(\text{King}) = \frac{4}{52} = \frac{1}{13}\)

But what if I tell you the card is a face card? Now:

The conditional probability of \(A\) given \(B\) is:
\[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad \text{provided } P(B) > 0\]
Read “\(P(A \mid B)\)” as “the probability of \(A\) given \(B\).”


Intuition: We’re restricting our attention to only the outcomes where \(B\) occurred, then asking what fraction of those also have \(A\).












Let’s apply the formula to our earlier example:

\(A\) = card is a King
\(B\) = card is a face card

Find each piece:

\(P(A \cap B) = P(\text{King AND face card}) = \frac{4}{52}\) (all Kings are face cards)
\(P(B) = P(\text{face card}) = \frac{12}{52}\)

Apply the formula:
\[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} = \frac{4/52}{12/52} = \frac{4}{12} = \frac{1}{3}\]
This matches our intuition: of the 12 face cards, 4 are Kings.

title: Lesson 7: Conditional Probability
text:
Rearranging the conditional probability formula:
\[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \implies P(A \cap B) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)\]






ImportantMultiplication Rule



\[P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)\]
The probability of \(A\) and \(B\) equals the probability of one event times the conditional probability of the other.











Reading the diagram:

First, find \(P(A)\) — the blue circle
Then, \(P(B \mid A)\) asks: “of those in A, what fraction are also in B?”
Multiplying gives \(P(A \cap B)\) — the purple intersection





Draw one card from a deck. What is the probability it is red AND a face card?
Let:

\(A\) = card is red
\(B\) = card is a face card

Using the multiplication rule:
\[P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)\]

\(P(A) = \frac{26}{52} = \frac{1}{2}\) (26 red cards)
\(P(B \mid A) = \frac{6}{26}\) (of the 26 red cards, 6 are face cards: J, Q, K of hearts and diamonds)

\[P(\text{red AND face card}) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6}{26} = \frac{6}{52} = \frac{3}{26} \approx 0.115\]
Check: There are 6 red face cards out of 52 total cards, so \(P = \frac{6}{52}\) ✓

title: Lesson 7: Conditional Probability
text:
Sometimes we want to find \(P(A)\), but it’s easier to compute \(P(A)\) by breaking it into cases based on another event \(B\).






ImportantLaw of Total Probability



If \(B\) and \(B^c\) partition the sample space (one must occur), then:
\[P(A) = P(A \cap B) + P(A \cap B^c)\]
Using the multiplication rule:
\[P(A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)\]















You have two boxes of cards:

Box 1: 10 cards — 4 are face cards, 6 are not
Box 2: 10 cards — 2 are face cards, 8 are not

You pick Box 1 with probability 0.70 and Box 2 with probability 0.30, then draw a card. What is P(face card)?
Let \(A\) = draw a face card, \(B\) = chose Box 1 (so \(B^c\) = chose Box 2)

\(P(B) = 0.70\), \(P(B^c) = 0.30\)
\(P(A \mid B) = \frac{4}{10} = 0.40\), \(P(A \mid B^c) = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20\)

\[P(A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)\] \[= 0.70 \times 0.40 + 0.30 \times 0.20\] \[= 0.28 + 0.06 = 0.34\]
34% of draws will be face cards.




For any partition \(B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\) of the sample space:
\[P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)\]

title: Lesson 7: Conditional Probability
text:
We often know \(P(A \mid B)\) but want \(P(B \mid A)\). Bayes’ Theorem lets us “flip” the conditioning.






ImportantBayes’ Theorem



\[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(A)}\]
Or using the Law of Total Probability in the denominator:
\[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)}\]






Using our two boxes example: if you drew a face card, what’s the probability it came from Box 1?
We have \(P(A \mid B)\) (probability of face card given Box 1), but we want \(P(B \mid A)\) (probability it was Box 1 given we drew a face card).
We know (using \(A\) = face card, \(B\) = Box 1):
- \(P(B) = 0.70\), \(P(B^c) = 0.30\)
- \(P(A \mid B) = 0.40\), \(P(A \mid B^c) = 0.20\)
- \(P(A) = 0.34\) (calculated earlier using Law of Total Probability)
Find \(P(B \mid A)\):
\[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)}\]
\[= \frac{0.70 \times 0.40}{0.70 \times 0.40 + 0.30 \times 0.20} = \frac{0.28}{0.28 + 0.06} = \frac{0.28}{0.34} \approx 0.824\]
About 82.4% of face cards came from Box 1. This is higher than 70% because Box 1 also has a higher proportion of face cards.






TipKey Insight



Bayes’ Theorem lets us “reverse” the conditional probability. We started knowing \(P(\text{face card} \mid \text{Box 1})\) and found \(P(\text{Box 1} \mid \text{face card})\).

title: Lesson 7: Conditional Probability
text:
A survey of 200 soldiers found:

140 have completed Airborne school
80 have completed Air Assault school
50 have completed both

A soldier is selected at random.






NoteQuestions






What is \(P(\text{Airborne})\)?
What is \(P(\text{Air Assault} \mid \text{Airborne})\)?
What is \(P(\text{Airborne} \mid \text{Air Assault})\)?
What is \(P(\text{Airborne AND Air Assault})\)?










TipAnswers






\(P(\text{Airborne}) = \frac{140}{200} = 0.70\)
\(P(\text{Air Assault} \mid \text{Airborne}) = \frac{P(\text{Both})}{P(\text{Airborne})} = \frac{50/200}{140/200} = \frac{50}{140} = \frac{5}{14} \approx 0.357\)
\(P(\text{Airborne} \mid \text{Air Assault}) = \frac{P(\text{Both})}{P(\text{Air Assault})} = \frac{50/200}{80/200} = \frac{50}{80} = \frac{5}{8} = 0.625\)
\(P(\text{Airborne AND Air Assault}) = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25\)








In a class of 30 cadets:

18 are in Engineering
12 take Statistics
6 are in Engineering AND take Statistics







NoteQuestions






What is \(P(\text{Statistics} \mid \text{Engineering})\)?
What is \(P(\text{Engineering} \mid \text{Statistics})\)?
What is \(P(\text{Engineering AND Statistics})\)?










TipAnswers





Let \(E\) = Engineering, \(S\) = Statistics

\[P(S \mid E) = \frac{P(S \cap E)}{P(E)} = \frac{6/30}{18/30} = \frac{6}{18} = \frac{1}{3}\]
\[P(E \mid S) = \frac{P(E \cap S)}{P(S)} = \frac{6/30}{12/30} = \frac{6}{12} = \frac{1}{2}\]
\[P(E \cap S) = \frac{6}{30} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20\]








A disease affects 2% of the population. A diagnostic test has:

Sensitivity: \(P(\text{positive} \mid \text{disease}) = 0.95\)
False positive rate: \(P(\text{positive} \mid \text{no disease}) = 0.08\)







NoteQuestions






What is \(P(\text{disease AND positive test})\)?
What is \(P(\text{no disease AND positive test})\)?
What is the overall probability of testing positive?










TipAnswers






Using the multiplication rule: \[P(D \cap +) = P(D) \cdot P(+ \mid D) = 0.02 \times 0.95 = 0.019\]
Using the multiplication rule: \[P(D^c \cap +) = P(D^c) \cdot P(+ \mid D^c) = 0.98 \times 0.08 = 0.0784\]
A positive test can happen with or without disease: \[P(+) = P(D \cap +) + P(D^c \cap +) = 0.019 + 0.0784 = 0.0974\]

About 9.74% of people will test positive.







At a checkpoint, 5% of vehicles are selected for inspection. Of the vehicles inspected, 2% are found to have violations. Of the vehicles not inspected, 0.1% have violations that go undetected.






NoteQuestions






What is the probability a randomly selected vehicle is inspected AND has a violation?
What is the probability a vehicle has a violation (detected or not)?










TipAnswers





Let \(I\) = inspected, \(V\) = has violation

Using the multiplication rule: \[P(I \cap V) = P(I) \cdot P(V \mid I) = 0.05 \times 0.02 = 0.001\]
Using the law of total probability (a vehicle either is or isn’t inspected): \[P(V) = P(I \cap V) + P(I^c \cap V)\] \[= P(I) \cdot P(V \mid I) + P(I^c) \cdot P(V \mid I^c)\] \[= 0.05 \times 0.02 + 0.95 \times 0.001\] \[= 0.001 + 0.00095 = 0.00195\]

About 0.195% of vehicles have violations.







A lock has a 4-digit code where each digit is 0-9. Assume all codes are equally likely.






NoteQuestions






How many possible codes are there?
If you guess randomly, what’s the probability of guessing correctly on the first try?
If you can try 3 times, what’s the probability of guessing correctly at least once?










TipAnswers






Each digit has 10 choices: \[10 \times 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 10^4 = 10,000 \text{ codes}\]
\[P(\text{correct on first try}) = \frac{1}{10000}\]
Using the complement (assuming you don’t repeat guesses): \[P(\text{at least one correct in 3 tries}) = 1 - P(\text{all 3 wrong})\]

If guessing without replacement from the 10,000 codes: \[P(\text{all wrong}) = \frac{9999}{10000} \cdot \frac{9998}{9999} \cdot \frac{9997}{9998} = \frac{9997}{10000}\]
\[P(\text{at least one correct}) = 1 - \frac{9997}{10000} = \frac{3}{10000} = 0.0003\]







You go on a date with Taylor Swift. Based on historical data:

30% of her dates go well
50% of her dates are meh
20% of her dates go badly

The probability Taylor writes a song about the date depends on how it went:

\(P(\text{song} \mid \text{well}) = 0.10\) (happy, less drama)
\(P(\text{song} \mid \text{meh}) = 0.30\) (some material)
\(P(\text{song} \mid \text{badly}) = 0.90\) (maximum inspiration)







NoteQuestions






What is the probability Taylor writes a song about your date?
If Taylor wrote a song about your date, what’s the probability it went badly?










TipAnswers





Let \(S\) = song written, \(W\) = went well, \(M\) = meh, \(B\) = went badly
a) Use Law of Total Probability:
\[P(S) = P(W) \cdot P(S \mid W) + P(M) \cdot P(S \mid M) + P(B) \cdot P(S \mid B)\] \[= 0.30 \times 0.10 + 0.50 \times 0.30 + 0.20 \times 0.90\] \[= 0.03 + 0.15 + 0.18 = 0.36\]
There’s a 36% chance she writes a song about your date.
b) Use Bayes’ Theorem:
\[P(B \mid S) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(S \mid B)}{P(S)} = \frac{0.20 \times 0.90}{0.36} = \frac{0.18}{0.36} = 0.50\]
If Taylor wrote a song about you, there’s a 50% chance the date went badly!
Even though only 20% of dates go badly, bad dates are so much more likely to become songs that they account for half of all songs written.

title: Lesson 7: Conditional Probability
text:
ImportantCore Concepts



Conditional Probability: \[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}\]
Multiplication Rule: \[P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)\]
Law of Total Probability: \[P(A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)\]
Bayes’ Theorem: \[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(A)}\]

title: Lesson 9: Discrete Random Variables
text:
NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 6



Sample Spaces and Events:

Sample space \(S\) = set of all possible outcomes
Event = subset of the sample space
Operations: Union (\(A \cup B\)), Intersection (\(A \cap B\)), Complement (\(A^c\))

Kolmogorov Axioms:

\(P(A) \geq 0\)
\(P(S) = 1\)
For mutually exclusive events: \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)\)

Key Rules:

Complement Rule: \(P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)\)
Addition Rule: \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)\)












NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 7



Conditional Probability: \[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}\]
Multiplication Rule: \[P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)\]
Law of Total Probability: \[P(A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)\]
Bayes’ Theorem: \[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)}\]











NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 8



Multiplication Principle: If an experiment has stages with \(n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k\) outcomes, the total outcomes are \(n_1 \times n_2 \times \cdots \times n_k\).
Counting Formulas:









With Replacement
Without Replacement




Ordered
\(n^k\)
\(P(n,k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!}\)


Unordered
\(\binom{n+k-1}{k}\)
\(\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}\)



Independence:

\(A\) and \(B\) are independent if \(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\)
Equivalently: \(P(A \mid B) = P(A)\)
Independent \(\neq\) Mutually Exclusive!











Understand how PMFs are created and used in discrete random variables
Understand how CDFs are used in discrete random variables
Understand and calculate Expected Value and Variance




Devore, Sections 3.1, 3.2, 3.3













  Your browser does not support the video tag. 











ImportantVantage



Let’s go to Vantage and walk through how to render and turn in your Exploratory Data Analysis assignment.























In probability, we often care about numerical summaries of random experiments rather than the outcomes themselves.






ImportantDefinition: Random Variable



A random variable is a function that assigns a numerical value to each outcome in a sample space.
\[
X: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}
\]
We use capital letters (\(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\)) for random variables and lowercase letters (\(x\), \(y\), \(z\)) for their specific values.


A random variable is simply a rule for turning outcomes into numbers.
Example 1
Select a cadet at random and let \(X = \text{height (in inches) of the selected cadet}.\)
If the cadet is 70 inches tall, then
\[
70 \rightarrow 70.
\]









Example 2
Toss two coins with outcomes \(HH, HT, TH, TT\). Define \(X = \text{number of heads}.\)
Then

\(TT \rightarrow 0\)
\(TH \rightarrow 1\)

\(HT \rightarrow 1\)

\(HH \rightarrow 2\)

In this case, the random variable explicitly converts symbolic outcomes into numbers.













Random variables come in two types:






NoteTypes of Random Variables



Discrete Random Variable:

Takes on a finite or countably infinite number of distinct values
Examples: Number of cadets absent, number of hits on target, count of defective items

Continuous Random Variable:

Takes on any value in an interval (uncountably infinite)
Examples: Time to complete a task, weight, distance, temperature









WarningToday’s Focus



Today we focus exclusively on discrete random variables.
We’ll cover continuous random variables in Lesson 12.






Throughout this lesson, we’ll use one example to build everything:






NoteExample: Coin Flips



A cadet flips a fair coin 3 times.
Let \(X\) = number of heads (out of 3 flips).
What values can \(X\) take? \(X \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}\)


We’ll use this example to teach the rest of the material for today.












ImportantDefinition: Probability Mass Function



The probability mass function (PMF) of a discrete random variable \(X\) is:
\[p(x) = P(X = x)\]
which gives the probability that \(X\) takes the value \(x\).
Properties:

\(p(x) \geq 0\) for all \(x\)
\(\sum_{\text{all } x} p(x) = 1\)












NoteHow did we build this PMF?





Or as a formula: \(p(x) = \frac{\binom{3}{x}}{2^3}, \quad x \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}\)
Setup: 3 coin flips, each equally likely to be H or T.
Total outcomes (ordered, with replacement): \(n^k = 2^3 = 8\) equally likely outcomes: \(\{HHH, HHT, HTH, HTT, THH, THT, TTH, TTT\}\)
Since all outcomes are equally likely, we use:
\[P(X = x) = \frac{\text{number of outcomes with } x \text{ heads}}{\text{total outcomes}} = \frac{\binom{3}{x}}{n^k} = \frac{\binom{3}{x}}{2^3}\]

Numerator: Unordered without replacement → \(\binom{3}{x}\) (choosing which flips are heads)
Denominator: Ordered with replacement → \(n^k = 2^3 = 8\) (each of 3 flips has 2 options)

Calculations:

\(P(X = 0) = \frac{\binom{3}{0}}{8} = \frac{1}{8}\) (just TTT)
\(P(X = 1) = \frac{\binom{3}{1}}{8} = \frac{3}{8}\) (HTT, THT, TTH)
\(P(X = 2) = \frac{\binom{3}{2}}{8} = \frac{3}{8}\) (HHT, HTH, THH)
\(P(X = 3) = \frac{\binom{3}{3}}{8} = \frac{1}{8}\) (just HHH)

Verify: All \(p(x) \geq 0\) ✓ and \(\sum p(x) = \frac{1+3+3+1}{8} = 1\) ✓



This uses counting! The numerator counts favorable outcomes, the denominator is the total number of equally likely outcomes.












\(x\)
0
1
2
3




\(p(x)\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)
\(\frac{3}{8}\)
\(\frac{3}{8}\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)















Note: For discrete RVs, we use spikes (not bars) to emphasize that probability exists only at specific points.



\[
p(X = x) = \begin{cases}
\frac{1}{8} & x = 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{8} & x = 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{8} & x = 2 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{8} & x = 3 \\[6pt]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\]





Now let’s use our PMF to answer some questions:






NoteQ1: What is the probability of getting 2 or fewer heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \leq 2) &= P(X = 0) + P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{3}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{7}{8}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ2: What is the probability of getting fewer than 2 heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X < 2) &= P(X = 0) + P(X = 1) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ3: What is the probability of getting exactly 2 heads?





\[P(X = 2) = \frac{3}{8}\]









NoteQ4: What is the probability of getting more than 2 heads?





\[P(X > 2) = P(X = 3) = \frac{1}{8}\]









NoteQ5: What is the probability of getting 2 or more heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq 2) &= P(X = 2) + P(X = 3) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{8} + \frac{1}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ6: What is the probability of getting between 1 and 3 heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(1 < X < 3) &= P(X = 2) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{8}
\end{aligned}
\]
















ImportantDefinition: Cumulative Distribution Function



The cumulative distribution function (CDF) of a random variable \(X\) is:
\[F(x) = P(X \leq x)\]
For a discrete RV, this is computed by summing the PMF:
\[F(x) = \sum_{y \leq x} p(y)\]


Key idea: The CDF accumulates (adds up) probabilities from the smallest value up to \(x\).




To build the CDF from the PMF, we accumulate probabilities:
\[F(x) = \sum_{y \leq x} p(y)\]
For our example:
\[
p(X = x) = \begin{cases}
\frac{1}{8} & x = 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{8} & x = 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{8} & x = 2 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{8} & x = 3 \\[6pt]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\]

\(F(0) = P(X \leq 0) = p(0) = \frac{1}{8}\)
\(F(1) = P(X \leq 1) = p(0) + p(1) = \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{8} = \frac{4}{8} = \frac{1}{2}\)
\(F(2) = P(X \leq 2) = p(0) + p(1) + p(2) = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{3}{8} = \frac{7}{8}\)
\(F(3) = P(X \leq 3) = p(0) + p(1) + p(2) + p(3) = \frac{7}{8} + \frac{1}{8} = 1\)















\(x\)
0
1
2
3




\(p(x)\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)
\(\frac{3}{8}\)
\(\frac{3}{8}\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)


\(F(x)\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)
\(\frac{1}{2}\)
\(\frac{7}{8}\)
\(1\)
















Note: The CDF of a discrete RV is a step function — it jumps at each possible value of \(X\).



\[
F(x) = \begin{cases}
0 & x < 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{8} & 0 \leq x < 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{2} & 1 \leq x < 2 \\[6pt]
\frac{7}{8} & 2 \leq x < 3 \\[6pt]
1 & x \geq 3
\end{cases}
\]




The CDF is useful for computing probabilities over ranges:






NoteQ1: What is the probability of getting 2 or fewer heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \leq 2) &= F(2) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{7}{8}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ2: What is the probability of getting fewer than 2 heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X < 2) &= P(X \leq 1) \\[6pt]
&= F(1) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ3: What is the probability of getting exactly 2 heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X = 2) &= F(2) - F(1) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{7}{8} - \frac{1}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{8}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ4: What is the probability of getting more than 2 heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X > 2) &= 1 - P(X \leq 2) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - F(2) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \frac{7}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{8}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ5: What is the probability of getting 2 or more heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq 2) &= 1 - P(X < 2) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - P(X \leq 1) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - F(1) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \frac{1}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ6: What is the probability of getting between 1 and 3 heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(1 < X < 3) &= F(2) - F(1) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{7}{8} - \frac{1}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{8}
\end{aligned}
\]







What if we wanted to know the ‘expected’ number of heads in our coin flip example?








ImportantDefinition: Expected Value



The expected value (or mean) of a discrete random variable \(X\) is:
\[E(X) = \mu = \sum_{\text{all } x} x \cdot p(x)\]


Interpretation: The expected value is the long-run average — if you repeated the experiment many times, the average value of \(X\) would approach \(E(X)\).






WarningImportant Note



The expected value is NOT necessarily a value that \(X\) can actually take!






\[E(X) = \sum_{x=0}^{3} x \cdot p(x)\]
\[= 0 \cdot \frac{1}{8} + 1 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 3 \cdot \frac{1}{8}\]
\[= 0 + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{6}{8} + \frac{3}{8} = \frac{12}{8} = 1.5\]
Interpretation: On average, you will get 1.5 heads out of 3 flips. Over many repetitions of this experiment, the average number of heads will approach 1.5.
Note: 1.5 is not a possible value of \(X\) (you can’t flip 1.5 heads), but it’s still a meaningful measure of center.





What if we wanted to know how much the number of heads varies between experiments from the expected value in our coin flip example?








ImportantDefinition: Variance



The variance of a discrete random variable \(X\) is:
\[Var(X) = \sigma^2 = E[(X - \mu)^2] = \sum_{\text{all } x} (x - \mu)^2 \cdot p(x)\]
Shortcut formula: \[Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2\]








ImportantDefinition: Standard Deviation



The standard deviation is:
\[SD(X) = \sigma = \sqrt{Var(X)}\]


Interpretation: Variance and SD measure how spread out the distribution is around the mean.






\[Var(X) = \sum_{\text{all } x} (x - \mu)^2 \cdot p(x)\]










\(x\)
0
1
2
3




\(p(x)\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)
\(\frac{3}{8}\)
\(\frac{3}{8}\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)



With \(\mu = 1.5\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
Var(X) &= (0 - 1.5)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{8} + (1 - 1.5)^2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + (2 - 1.5)^2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + (3 - 1.5)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{8} \\[6pt]
&= (2.25) \cdot \frac{1}{8} + (0.25) \cdot \frac{3}{8} + (0.25) \cdot \frac{3}{8} + (2.25) \cdot \frac{1}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2.25}{8} + \frac{0.75}{8} + \frac{0.75}{8} + \frac{2.25}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{6}{8} = 0.75
\end{aligned}
\]



\[Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2\]
Step 1: Find \(E(X^2)\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
E(X^2) &= \sum_{x=0}^{3} x^2 \cdot p(x) \\[6pt]
&= 0^2 \cdot \frac{1}{8} + 1^2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 2^2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 3^2 \cdot \frac{1}{8} \\[6pt]
&= 0 + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{12}{8} + \frac{9}{8} = \frac{24}{8} = 3
\end{aligned}
\]
Step 2: Apply the shortcut formula
\[Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = 3 - (1.5)^2 = 3 - 2.25 = 0.75\]




\[SD(X) = \sqrt{Var(X)} = \sqrt{0.75} \approx 0.866\]
Interpretation: The number of heads typically varies by about 0.87 from the average of 1.5.










NoteComplete Analysis: 3 Fair Coin Flips



PMF:
\[
p(x) = \begin{cases}
\frac{1}{8} & x = 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{8} & x = 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{8} & x = 2 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{8} & x = 3 \\[6pt]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\]
CDF:
\[
F(x) = \begin{cases}
0 & x < 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{8} & 0 \leq x < 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{2} & 1 \leq x < 2 \\[6pt]
\frac{7}{8} & 2 \leq x < 3 \\[6pt]
1 & x \geq 3
\end{cases}
\]
Summary Statistics:

\(E(X) = 1.5\) heads
\(Var(X) = 0.75\)
\(SD(X) \approx 0.866\) heads








A cadet rolls a fair six-sided die. Let \(X\) = the number showing on the top face.








NoteA1: What values can X take?





\(X \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}\)









NoteA2: Find the PMF in tabular form

















\(x\)
1
2
3
4
5
6




\(p(x)\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)












NoteA3: Write the PMF as a formula





\[
p(x) = \begin{cases}
\frac{1}{6} & x \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\} \\[6pt]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\]









NoteA4: Sketch the PMF



























NoteB1: Find the CDF in tabular form

















\(x\)
1
2
3
4
5
6




\(F(x)\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)
\(\frac{2}{6}\)
\(\frac{3}{6}\)
\(\frac{4}{6}\)
\(\frac{5}{6}\)
\(1\)












NoteB2: Write the CDF as a formula





\[
F(x) = \begin{cases}
0 & x < 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{6} & 1 \leq x < 2 \\[6pt]
\frac{2}{6} & 2 \leq x < 3 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{6} & 3 \leq x < 4 \\[6pt]
\frac{4}{6} & 4 \leq x < 5 \\[6pt]
\frac{5}{6} & 5 \leq x < 6 \\[6pt]
1 & x \geq 6
\end{cases}
\]









NoteB3: Sketch the CDF



























NoteC1: What is the probability of rolling 4 or fewer?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \leq 4) &= P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) + P(X = 3) + P(X = 4) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteC2: What is the probability of rolling fewer than 4?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X < 4) &= P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) + P(X = 3) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteC3: What is the probability of rolling exactly 4?





\[P(X = 4) = \frac{1}{6}\]









NoteC4: What is the probability of rolling more than 4?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X > 4) &= P(X = 5) + P(X = 6) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteC5: What is the probability of rolling 4 or more?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq 4) &= P(X = 4) + P(X = 5) + P(X = 6) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteC6: What is the probability of rolling between 2 and 5?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(2 \leq X \leq 5) &= P(X = 2) + P(X = 3) + P(X = 4) + P(X = 5) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
\]













NoteD1: What is the probability of rolling 4 or fewer?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \leq 4) &= F(4) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteD2: What is the probability of rolling fewer than 4?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X < 4) &= P(X \leq 3) \\[6pt]
&= F(3) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteD3: What is the probability of rolling exactly 4?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X = 4) &= F(4) - F(3) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4}{6} - \frac{3}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{6}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteD4: What is the probability of rolling more than 4?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X > 4) &= 1 - P(X \leq 4) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - F(4) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \frac{4}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteD5: What is the probability of rolling 4 or more?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq 4) &= 1 - P(X < 4) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - P(X \leq 3) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - F(3) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \frac{3}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteD6: What is the probability of rolling between 2 and 5?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(2 \leq X \leq 5) &= F(5) - F(1) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{5}{6} - \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
\]













NoteE1: Calculate E(X)





\[
\begin{aligned}
E(X) &= \sum_{x=1}^{6} x \cdot p(x) \\[6pt]
&= 1 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 3 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 4 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 5 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 6 \cdot \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{21}{6} = 3.5
\end{aligned}
\]
Interpretation: On average, the die will show 3.5 over many rolls.













NoteF1: Calculate Var(X) using the shortcut formula





Step 1: Find \(E(X^2)\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
E(X^2) &= \sum_{x=1}^{6} x^2 \cdot p(x) \\[6pt]
&= 1^2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 2^2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 3^2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 4^2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 5^2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 6^2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 36}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{91}{6} \approx 15.167
\end{aligned}
\]
Step 2: Apply the shortcut formula
\[
\begin{aligned}
Var(X) &= E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{91}{6} - (3.5)^2 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{91}{6} - \frac{73.5}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{17.5}{6} \approx 2.917
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteF2: Calculate SD(X)





\[SD(X) = \sqrt{Var(X)} = \sqrt{\frac{17.5}{6}} \approx 1.708\]
Interpretation: The die roll typically varies by about 1.7 from the average of 3.5.











A random variable maps outcomes to numbers
Discrete RVs take finite/countable values
PMF: \(p(x) = P(X = x)\) with properties \(p(x) \geq 0\) and \(\sum p(x) = 1\)
CDF: \(F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \sum_{y \leq x} p(y)\)
Expected Value: \(E(X) = \sum x \cdot p(x)\) — the long-run average
Variance: \(Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2\) — measures spread

Any questions?

Compute binomial PMF/CDF values
Interpret binomial mean and variance

title: Lesson 9: Discrete Random Variables
text:
NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 6



Sample Spaces and Events:

Sample space \(S\) = set of all possible outcomes
Event = subset of the sample space
Operations: Union (\(A \cup B\)), Intersection (\(A \cap B\)), Complement (\(A^c\))

Kolmogorov Axioms:

\(P(A) \geq 0\)
\(P(S) = 1\)
For mutually exclusive events: \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)\)

Key Rules:

Complement Rule: \(P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)\)
Addition Rule: \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)\)












NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 7



Conditional Probability: \[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}\]
Multiplication Rule: \[P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)\]
Law of Total Probability: \[P(A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)\]
Bayes’ Theorem: \[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) + P(B^c) \cdot P(A \mid B^c)}\]











NoteKey Concepts from Lesson 8



Multiplication Principle: If an experiment has stages with \(n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k\) outcomes, the total outcomes are \(n_1 \times n_2 \times \cdots \times n_k\).
Counting Formulas:









With Replacement
Without Replacement




Ordered
\(n^k\)
\(P(n,k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!}\)


Unordered
\(\binom{n+k-1}{k}\)
\(\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}\)



Independence:

\(A\) and \(B\) are independent if \(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\)
Equivalently: \(P(A \mid B) = P(A)\)
Independent \(\neq\) Mutually Exclusive!

title: Lesson 9: Discrete Random Variables
text:
In probability, we often care about numerical summaries of random experiments rather than the outcomes themselves.






ImportantDefinition: Random Variable



A random variable is a function that assigns a numerical value to each outcome in a sample space.
\[
X: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}
\]
We use capital letters (\(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\)) for random variables and lowercase letters (\(x\), \(y\), \(z\)) for their specific values.


A random variable is simply a rule for turning outcomes into numbers.
Example 1
Select a cadet at random and let \(X = \text{height (in inches) of the selected cadet}.\)
If the cadet is 70 inches tall, then
\[
70 \rightarrow 70.
\]









Example 2
Toss two coins with outcomes \(HH, HT, TH, TT\). Define \(X = \text{number of heads}.\)
Then

\(TT \rightarrow 0\)
\(TH \rightarrow 1\)

\(HT \rightarrow 1\)

\(HH \rightarrow 2\)

In this case, the random variable explicitly converts symbolic outcomes into numbers.

title: Lesson 9: Discrete Random Variables
text:
Random variables come in two types:






NoteTypes of Random Variables



Discrete Random Variable:

Takes on a finite or countably infinite number of distinct values
Examples: Number of cadets absent, number of hits on target, count of defective items

Continuous Random Variable:

Takes on any value in an interval (uncountably infinite)
Examples: Time to complete a task, weight, distance, temperature









WarningToday’s Focus



Today we focus exclusively on discrete random variables.
We’ll cover continuous random variables in Lesson 12.

title: Lesson 9: Discrete Random Variables
text:
Throughout this lesson, we’ll use one example to build everything:






NoteExample: Coin Flips



A cadet flips a fair coin 3 times.
Let \(X\) = number of heads (out of 3 flips).
What values can \(X\) take? \(X \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}\)


We’ll use this example to teach the rest of the material for today.

title: Lesson 9: Discrete Random Variables
text:
ImportantDefinition: Probability Mass Function



The probability mass function (PMF) of a discrete random variable \(X\) is:
\[p(x) = P(X = x)\]
which gives the probability that \(X\) takes the value \(x\).
Properties:

\(p(x) \geq 0\) for all \(x\)
\(\sum_{\text{all } x} p(x) = 1\)












NoteHow did we build this PMF?





Or as a formula: \(p(x) = \frac{\binom{3}{x}}{2^3}, \quad x \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}\)
Setup: 3 coin flips, each equally likely to be H or T.
Total outcomes (ordered, with replacement): \(n^k = 2^3 = 8\) equally likely outcomes: \(\{HHH, HHT, HTH, HTT, THH, THT, TTH, TTT\}\)
Since all outcomes are equally likely, we use:
\[P(X = x) = \frac{\text{number of outcomes with } x \text{ heads}}{\text{total outcomes}} = \frac{\binom{3}{x}}{n^k} = \frac{\binom{3}{x}}{2^3}\]

Numerator: Unordered without replacement → \(\binom{3}{x}\) (choosing which flips are heads)
Denominator: Ordered with replacement → \(n^k = 2^3 = 8\) (each of 3 flips has 2 options)

Calculations:

\(P(X = 0) = \frac{\binom{3}{0}}{8} = \frac{1}{8}\) (just TTT)
\(P(X = 1) = \frac{\binom{3}{1}}{8} = \frac{3}{8}\) (HTT, THT, TTH)
\(P(X = 2) = \frac{\binom{3}{2}}{8} = \frac{3}{8}\) (HHT, HTH, THH)
\(P(X = 3) = \frac{\binom{3}{3}}{8} = \frac{1}{8}\) (just HHH)

Verify: All \(p(x) \geq 0\) ✓ and \(\sum p(x) = \frac{1+3+3+1}{8} = 1\) ✓



This uses counting! The numerator counts favorable outcomes, the denominator is the total number of equally likely outcomes.












\(x\)
0
1
2
3




\(p(x)\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)
\(\frac{3}{8}\)
\(\frac{3}{8}\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)















Note: For discrete RVs, we use spikes (not bars) to emphasize that probability exists only at specific points.



\[
p(X = x) = \begin{cases}
\frac{1}{8} & x = 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{8} & x = 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{8} & x = 2 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{8} & x = 3 \\[6pt]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\]





Now let’s use our PMF to answer some questions:






NoteQ1: What is the probability of getting 2 or fewer heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \leq 2) &= P(X = 0) + P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{3}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{7}{8}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ2: What is the probability of getting fewer than 2 heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X < 2) &= P(X = 0) + P(X = 1) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ3: What is the probability of getting exactly 2 heads?





\[P(X = 2) = \frac{3}{8}\]









NoteQ4: What is the probability of getting more than 2 heads?





\[P(X > 2) = P(X = 3) = \frac{1}{8}\]









NoteQ5: What is the probability of getting 2 or more heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq 2) &= P(X = 2) + P(X = 3) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{8} + \frac{1}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ6: What is the probability of getting between 1 and 3 heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(1 < X < 3) &= P(X = 2) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{8}
\end{aligned}
\]

title: Lesson 9: Discrete Random Variables
text:
ImportantDefinition: Cumulative Distribution Function



The cumulative distribution function (CDF) of a random variable \(X\) is:
\[F(x) = P(X \leq x)\]
For a discrete RV, this is computed by summing the PMF:
\[F(x) = \sum_{y \leq x} p(y)\]


Key idea: The CDF accumulates (adds up) probabilities from the smallest value up to \(x\).




To build the CDF from the PMF, we accumulate probabilities:
\[F(x) = \sum_{y \leq x} p(y)\]
For our example:
\[
p(X = x) = \begin{cases}
\frac{1}{8} & x = 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{8} & x = 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{8} & x = 2 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{8} & x = 3 \\[6pt]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\]

\(F(0) = P(X \leq 0) = p(0) = \frac{1}{8}\)
\(F(1) = P(X \leq 1) = p(0) + p(1) = \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{8} = \frac{4}{8} = \frac{1}{2}\)
\(F(2) = P(X \leq 2) = p(0) + p(1) + p(2) = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{3}{8} = \frac{7}{8}\)
\(F(3) = P(X \leq 3) = p(0) + p(1) + p(2) + p(3) = \frac{7}{8} + \frac{1}{8} = 1\)















\(x\)
0
1
2
3




\(p(x)\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)
\(\frac{3}{8}\)
\(\frac{3}{8}\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)


\(F(x)\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)
\(\frac{1}{2}\)
\(\frac{7}{8}\)
\(1\)
















Note: The CDF of a discrete RV is a step function — it jumps at each possible value of \(X\).



\[
F(x) = \begin{cases}
0 & x < 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{8} & 0 \leq x < 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{2} & 1 \leq x < 2 \\[6pt]
\frac{7}{8} & 2 \leq x < 3 \\[6pt]
1 & x \geq 3
\end{cases}
\]




The CDF is useful for computing probabilities over ranges:






NoteQ1: What is the probability of getting 2 or fewer heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \leq 2) &= F(2) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{7}{8}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ2: What is the probability of getting fewer than 2 heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X < 2) &= P(X \leq 1) \\[6pt]
&= F(1) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ3: What is the probability of getting exactly 2 heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X = 2) &= F(2) - F(1) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{7}{8} - \frac{1}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{8}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ4: What is the probability of getting more than 2 heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X > 2) &= 1 - P(X \leq 2) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - F(2) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \frac{7}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{8}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ5: What is the probability of getting 2 or more heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq 2) &= 1 - P(X < 2) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - P(X \leq 1) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - F(1) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \frac{1}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteQ6: What is the probability of getting between 1 and 3 heads?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(1 < X < 3) &= F(2) - F(1) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{7}{8} - \frac{1}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{8}
\end{aligned}
\]

title: Lesson 9: Discrete Random Variables
text:
What if we wanted to know the ‘expected’ number of heads in our coin flip example?








ImportantDefinition: Expected Value



The expected value (or mean) of a discrete random variable \(X\) is:
\[E(X) = \mu = \sum_{\text{all } x} x \cdot p(x)\]


Interpretation: The expected value is the long-run average — if you repeated the experiment many times, the average value of \(X\) would approach \(E(X)\).






WarningImportant Note



The expected value is NOT necessarily a value that \(X\) can actually take!






\[E(X) = \sum_{x=0}^{3} x \cdot p(x)\]
\[= 0 \cdot \frac{1}{8} + 1 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 3 \cdot \frac{1}{8}\]
\[= 0 + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{6}{8} + \frac{3}{8} = \frac{12}{8} = 1.5\]
Interpretation: On average, you will get 1.5 heads out of 3 flips. Over many repetitions of this experiment, the average number of heads will approach 1.5.
Note: 1.5 is not a possible value of \(X\) (you can’t flip 1.5 heads), but it’s still a meaningful measure of center.

title: Lesson 9: Discrete Random Variables
text:
What if we wanted to know how much the number of heads varies between experiments from the expected value in our coin flip example?








ImportantDefinition: Variance



The variance of a discrete random variable \(X\) is:
\[Var(X) = \sigma^2 = E[(X - \mu)^2] = \sum_{\text{all } x} (x - \mu)^2 \cdot p(x)\]
Shortcut formula: \[Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2\]








ImportantDefinition: Standard Deviation



The standard deviation is:
\[SD(X) = \sigma = \sqrt{Var(X)}\]


Interpretation: Variance and SD measure how spread out the distribution is around the mean.






\[Var(X) = \sum_{\text{all } x} (x - \mu)^2 \cdot p(x)\]










\(x\)
0
1
2
3




\(p(x)\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)
\(\frac{3}{8}\)
\(\frac{3}{8}\)
\(\frac{1}{8}\)



With \(\mu = 1.5\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
Var(X) &= (0 - 1.5)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{8} + (1 - 1.5)^2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + (2 - 1.5)^2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + (3 - 1.5)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{8} \\[6pt]
&= (2.25) \cdot \frac{1}{8} + (0.25) \cdot \frac{3}{8} + (0.25) \cdot \frac{3}{8} + (2.25) \cdot \frac{1}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2.25}{8} + \frac{0.75}{8} + \frac{0.75}{8} + \frac{2.25}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{6}{8} = 0.75
\end{aligned}
\]



\[Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2\]
Step 1: Find \(E(X^2)\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
E(X^2) &= \sum_{x=0}^{3} x^2 \cdot p(x) \\[6pt]
&= 0^2 \cdot \frac{1}{8} + 1^2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 2^2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 3^2 \cdot \frac{1}{8} \\[6pt]
&= 0 + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{12}{8} + \frac{9}{8} = \frac{24}{8} = 3
\end{aligned}
\]
Step 2: Apply the shortcut formula
\[Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = 3 - (1.5)^2 = 3 - 2.25 = 0.75\]




\[SD(X) = \sqrt{Var(X)} = \sqrt{0.75} \approx 0.866\]
Interpretation: The number of heads typically varies by about 0.87 from the average of 1.5.










NoteComplete Analysis: 3 Fair Coin Flips



PMF:
\[
p(x) = \begin{cases}
\frac{1}{8} & x = 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{8} & x = 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{8} & x = 2 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{8} & x = 3 \\[6pt]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\]
CDF:
\[
F(x) = \begin{cases}
0 & x < 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{8} & 0 \leq x < 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{2} & 1 \leq x < 2 \\[6pt]
\frac{7}{8} & 2 \leq x < 3 \\[6pt]
1 & x \geq 3
\end{cases}
\]
Summary Statistics:

\(E(X) = 1.5\) heads
\(Var(X) = 0.75\)
\(SD(X) \approx 0.866\) heads

title: Lesson 9: Discrete Random Variables
text:
A cadet rolls a fair six-sided die. Let \(X\) = the number showing on the top face.








NoteA1: What values can X take?





\(X \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}\)









NoteA2: Find the PMF in tabular form

















\(x\)
1
2
3
4
5
6




\(p(x)\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)












NoteA3: Write the PMF as a formula





\[
p(x) = \begin{cases}
\frac{1}{6} & x \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\} \\[6pt]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\]









NoteA4: Sketch the PMF



























NoteB1: Find the CDF in tabular form

















\(x\)
1
2
3
4
5
6




\(F(x)\)
\(\frac{1}{6}\)
\(\frac{2}{6}\)
\(\frac{3}{6}\)
\(\frac{4}{6}\)
\(\frac{5}{6}\)
\(1\)












NoteB2: Write the CDF as a formula





\[
F(x) = \begin{cases}
0 & x < 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{1}{6} & 1 \leq x < 2 \\[6pt]
\frac{2}{6} & 2 \leq x < 3 \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{6} & 3 \leq x < 4 \\[6pt]
\frac{4}{6} & 4 \leq x < 5 \\[6pt]
\frac{5}{6} & 5 \leq x < 6 \\[6pt]
1 & x \geq 6
\end{cases}
\]









NoteB3: Sketch the CDF



























NoteC1: What is the probability of rolling 4 or fewer?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \leq 4) &= P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) + P(X = 3) + P(X = 4) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteC2: What is the probability of rolling fewer than 4?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X < 4) &= P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) + P(X = 3) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteC3: What is the probability of rolling exactly 4?





\[P(X = 4) = \frac{1}{6}\]









NoteC4: What is the probability of rolling more than 4?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X > 4) &= P(X = 5) + P(X = 6) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteC5: What is the probability of rolling 4 or more?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq 4) &= P(X = 4) + P(X = 5) + P(X = 6) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteC6: What is the probability of rolling between 2 and 5?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(2 \leq X \leq 5) &= P(X = 2) + P(X = 3) + P(X = 4) + P(X = 5) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
\]













NoteD1: What is the probability of rolling 4 or fewer?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \leq 4) &= F(4) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteD2: What is the probability of rolling fewer than 4?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X < 4) &= P(X \leq 3) \\[6pt]
&= F(3) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteD3: What is the probability of rolling exactly 4?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X = 4) &= F(4) - F(3) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4}{6} - \frac{3}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{6}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteD4: What is the probability of rolling more than 4?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X > 4) &= 1 - P(X \leq 4) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - F(4) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \frac{4}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteD5: What is the probability of rolling 4 or more?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq 4) &= 1 - P(X < 4) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - P(X \leq 3) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - F(3) \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \frac{3}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteD6: What is the probability of rolling between 2 and 5?





\[
\begin{aligned}
P(2 \leq X \leq 5) &= F(5) - F(1) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{5}{6} - \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
\]













NoteE1: Calculate E(X)





\[
\begin{aligned}
E(X) &= \sum_{x=1}^{6} x \cdot p(x) \\[6pt]
&= 1 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 3 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 4 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 5 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 6 \cdot \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{21}{6} = 3.5
\end{aligned}
\]
Interpretation: On average, the die will show 3.5 over many rolls.













NoteF1: Calculate Var(X) using the shortcut formula





Step 1: Find \(E(X^2)\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
E(X^2) &= \sum_{x=1}^{6} x^2 \cdot p(x) \\[6pt]
&= 1^2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 2^2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 3^2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 4^2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 5^2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 6^2 \cdot \frac{1}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 36}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{91}{6} \approx 15.167
\end{aligned}
\]
Step 2: Apply the shortcut formula
\[
\begin{aligned}
Var(X) &= E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{91}{6} - (3.5)^2 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{91}{6} - \frac{73.5}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{17.5}{6} \approx 2.917
\end{aligned}
\]









NoteF2: Calculate SD(X)





\[SD(X) = \sqrt{Var(X)} = \sqrt{\frac{17.5}{6}} \approx 1.708\]
Interpretation: The die roll typically varies by about 1.7 from the average of 3.5.

title: Lesson 9: Discrete Random Variables
text:
A random variable maps outcomes to numbers
Discrete RVs take finite/countable values
PMF: \(p(x) = P(X = x)\) with properties \(p(x) \geq 0\) and \(\sum p(x) = 1\)
CDF: \(F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \sum_{y \leq x} p(y)\)
Expected Value: \(E(X) = \sum x \cdot p(x)\) — the long-run average
Variance: \(Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2\) — measures spread

Any questions?




Lesson 10: Binomial Distribution

Verify binomial conditions for counts of successes
Compute binomial PMF/CDF values
Interpret binomial mean and variance






WebAssign 3.1, 3.2, 3.3 - Due before Lesson 10
Exploratory Data Analysis - Due Lesson 9
WPR I - Lesson 16

title: Week 1: Unboxing – Raspberry Pi & Linux
text:
Today we go from a box of parts to a working computer. By the end of this lesson you will have a functioning Raspberry Pi running Linux, and you’ll be comfortable navigating the file system from the command line.










NoteBy the end of this lesson, you will be able to:




Set up a Raspberry Pi with Raspberry Pi OS
Navigate the Linux file system using the terminal (ls, cd, pwd, mkdir, rm)
Understand file permissions and use chmod
SSH into your Pi remotely from another machine
Install packages with apt
Use piping (|) and redirection (>, >>)







Most data science courses hand you a pre-configured laptop with everything installed. That’s convenient, but it hides the infrastructure that makes everything work.
By starting with a Raspberry Pi, you’ll understand:

What an operating system actually does
How software gets installed
How remote access works (you’ll SSH into servers your whole career)
What happens “under the hood” when you run R or Python








Raspberry Pi board
MicroSD card
Power supply
Case (optional but recommended)
Keyboard, mouse, monitor (temporary – we’ll SSH soon)





Download the Raspberry Pi Imager on a lab computer
Insert the MicroSD card
Select Raspberry Pi OS (64-bit)
Set hostname, username, password, and Wi-Fi during the advanced options step
Flash and insert the card into the Pi
Power on – first boot takes a few minutes






Once you’re at the desktop (or logged in via terminal), open a terminal window.


pwd           # print working directory -- where am I?
ls            # list files in current directory
ls -la        # list all files (including hidden) with details
cd Documents  # change into Documents directory
cd ..         # go up one directory
cd ~          # go to home directory
mkdir mydir   # create a new directory
rm file.txt   # remove a file
rm -r mydir   # remove a directory and its contents



/              # root of the entire file system
├── home/      # user home directories
│   └── pi/    # your home directory
├── etc/       # system configuration files
├── var/       # variable data (logs, etc.)
├── usr/       # user programs
└── tmp/       # temporary files






TipPractice




Create a directory called ma491 in your home folder
Inside it, create subdirectories for data, scripts, and output
Use ls -la to verify your work
Use pwd to confirm where you are








Every file has three sets of permissions: owner, group, others.
Each set can have read (r), write (w), and execute (x).
ls -la
# -rw-r--r-- 1 pi pi 1024 Jan 10 09:00 notes.txt
#  ↑         owner  group
#  permissions
chmod 755 script.sh   # owner: rwx, group: r-x, others: r-x
chmod +x script.sh    # add execute permission for everyone




SSH (Secure Shell) lets you access your Pi from any other computer on the same network.
# On the Pi, check SSH is enabled:
sudo systemctl status ssh

# From your laptop:
ssh pi@<your-pi-ip-address>






TipFind Your Pi’s IP



On the Pi, run:
hostname -I


Once connected via SSH, you can unplug the monitor, keyboard, and mouse. Your Pi is now a headless server – just like real infrastructure.




apt is the package manager for Debian-based Linux (including Raspberry Pi OS).
sudo apt update          # refresh the list of available packages
sudo apt upgrade         # upgrade all installed packages
sudo apt install htop    # install a new package (htop = process viewer)




Piping sends the output of one command into another:
ls -la | head -5         # show only first 5 lines of ls output
cat /etc/passwd | grep pi # find lines containing "pi"
history | grep ssh       # find SSH commands you've run
Redirection sends output to a file:
echo "Hello MA491" > hello.txt    # write to file (overwrite)
echo "Another line" >> hello.txt  # append to file
ls -la > filelist.txt             # save directory listing to a file







Raspberry Pi is set up and running
You can navigate the file system from the terminal
You can SSH into your Pi from your laptop
You can install packages and use piping/redirection




Week 2: R & RStudio on the Pi

Install R and RStudio Server on the Pi
Access RStudio through your browser
R basics: vectors, data frames, functions, packages

title: Week 11: Guided Example – Spatial Data & Mapping
text:
Location matters. Where events happen, where resources are, where people live – spatial data adds a dimension that tables and charts can’t capture. This week you’ll work with geospatial data in R and build interactive maps.










NoteBy the end of this lesson, you will be able to:




Read and manipulate spatial data with the sf package
Build interactive maps with leaflet
Understand coordinate reference systems (CRS)
Create choropleth maps
Join spatial and tabular data
Apply spatial analysis to militarily-relevant problems







The sf (simple features) package treats spatial data as data frames with a geometry column.

library(tidyverse)
library(sf)

# Read a shapefile
states <- st_read("data/us_states.shp")

# Or use built-in data
nc <- st_read(system.file("shape/nc.shp", package = "sf"))

# It's a data frame with a geometry column
glimpse(nc)

# Plot it
ggplot(nc) +
  geom_sf() +
  theme_minimal()





Every spatial dataset has a CRS that defines how coordinates map to the Earth’s surface.

# Check the CRS
st_crs(nc)

# Transform to a different CRS
nc_wgs84 <- st_transform(nc, crs = 4326)  # WGS84 (lat/lon)
nc_albers <- st_transform(nc, crs = 5070) # Albers Equal Area







TipCommon CRS Codes






EPSG
Name
Use




4326
WGS84
GPS coordinates (lat/lon)


3857
Web Mercator
Web maps (Google, Leaflet)


5070
Albers Equal Area
US thematic maps










library(leaflet)

leaflet() |>
  addTiles() |>
  addMarkers(lng = -73.96, lat = 41.39,
             popup = "West Point") |>
  setView(lng = -73.96, lat = 41.39, zoom = 14)




# Example: Army installations
installations <- data.frame(
  name = c("West Point", "Fort Liberty", "Fort Cavazos"),
  lat = c(41.39, 35.14, 31.13),
  lng = c(-73.96, -79.01, -97.78)
)

leaflet(installations) |>
  addTiles() |>
  addCircleMarkers(~lng, ~lat, popup = ~name,
                   radius = 8, color = "darkgreen",
                   fillOpacity = 0.8)






Color regions by a variable:

# Choropleth of North Carolina births
ggplot(nc, aes(fill = BIR74)) +
  geom_sf() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c() +
  labs(title = "Births by County (1974)",
       fill = "Births") +
  theme_minimal()

# Interactive choropleth with leaflet
pal <- colorNumeric("viridis", domain = nc$BIR74)

leaflet(nc |> st_transform(4326)) |>
  addTiles() |>
  addPolygons(fillColor = ~pal(BIR74),
              fillOpacity = 0.7,
              weight = 1,
              color = "white",
              popup = ~paste(NAME, "<br>Births:", BIR74)) |>
  addLegend(pal = pal, values = ~BIR74, title = "Births (1974)")





Combine spatial data with tabular data:

# Join a data frame to a spatial object
county_stats <- data.frame(
  NAME = c("Ashe", "Alleghany", "Surry"),
  unemployment = c(5.2, 4.8, 6.1)
)

nc |>
  left_join(county_stats, by = "NAME") |>
  filter(!is.na(unemployment)) |>
  ggplot(aes(fill = unemployment)) +
  geom_sf() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c()

# Spatial join: which points fall within which polygons?
points_in_counties <- st_join(points_sf, nc)











TipIdeas




Map military installations with attributes (size, personnel, budget)
Analyze spatial patterns of incidents or events
Create operational overlays on terrain maps
Visualize logistics networks and supply routes
Map population density for civil affairs planning










You can read, manipulate, and visualize spatial data
You can build interactive maps with leaflet
You understand CRS and spatial joins




Week 12: Guided Example – Time Series & Forecasting

Time series structure and decomposition
Forecasting with tsibble + fable
ARIMA basics

title: Week 13: Capstone Work Session 1
text:
The guided examples are done. For the remaining five weeks, you’ll apply everything you’ve learned to your own individual project. This week: get your data, clean it, and begin exploring.










NoteGoals for Work Session 1




Finalize your data source and acquire the data
Load the data into R and perform initial cleaning
Begin exploratory data analysis (EDA)
Set up your project GitHub repository with a clean structure







Your GitHub repo should look like this:
ma491-capstone/
├── README.md
├── data/
│   ├── raw/           # original, untouched data files
│   └── clean/         # processed data
├── scripts/
│   ├── 01-acquire.R   # data acquisition
│   ├── 02-clean.R     # data cleaning
│   ├── 03-eda.R       # exploratory analysis
│   ├── 04-model.R     # modeling
│   └── 05-app.R       # Shiny app (or app/ directory)
├── report.qmd         # Quarto report
└── .gitignore





Data acquired and saved to data/raw/
Data loaded into R successfully
Initial cleaning script written (02-clean.R)
glimpse() and summary() run on the clean data
At least 3 exploratory visualizations created
GitHub repo created and initial commit pushed
Met with instructor to discuss progress





Today we’ll do an informal code review. Pair up and:

Pull your partner’s repo
Run their data loading script – does it work?
Read their code – is it clear? Any suggestions?
Give constructive feedback





During check-ins, be ready to discuss:

Is your data sufficient to answer your question?
Any data quality issues (missing values, unexpected formats)?
What patterns are emerging from your EDA?
What modeling approach are you planning?








Data is acquired and loaded
Initial EDA is underway
GitHub repo is set up




Week 14: Capstone Work Session 2

Continue analysis: modeling and results
Begin drafting your Quarto report

title: Week 15: Capstone Work Session 3
text:
This week you’ll build the Shiny app component of your capstone project. The app should let a user interact with your analysis – explore the data, adjust inputs, and see results update dynamically.










NoteGoals for Work Session 3




Build a Shiny app for your project
Include interactive elements that let users explore your analysis
Continue iterating on modeling and writing
Commit all progress to GitHub







Your app should include:

At least 2 interactive inputs (dropdowns, sliders, etc.)
At least 2 reactive outputs (plots, tables, text)
Clear layout with a title and organized panels
Meaningful interactivity – the app should reveal something about your analysis that a static report can’t







TipApp Ideas




Let users filter data by category and see updated visualizations
Let users choose which variables to plot or model
Show predictions for user-specified inputs
Display an interactive map that responds to selections
Show sentiment or text analysis results for user-selected documents








# app.R (single-file app)
library(shiny)
library(tidyverse)
library(bslib)

# Load your cleaned data
df <- readRDS("data/clean/my_data.rds")

ui <- page_sidebar(
  title = "Your Project Title",
  theme = bs_theme(bootswatch = "flatly"),
  sidebar = sidebar(
    # Your inputs here
  ),
  # Your outputs here
)

server <- function(input, output) {
  # Your reactive logic here
}

shinyApp(ui, server)






Shiny app runs locally without errors
At least 2 inputs and 2 outputs
App communicates something meaningful about your analysis
Analysis is refined based on findings
Quarto report updated with Methods and Results sections
All code committed to GitHub








Shiny app is functional
Analysis is nearing completion

Finalize everything: report, app, repo
Practice your presentation

title: Week 17: Capstone Presentations
text:
This is it. Each cadet presents their end-to-end data science project to the class. You’ll demonstrate everything you’ve built over the semester: from setting up a Raspberry Pi to delivering a complete analysis with a Shiny app.










NoteRequirements




10-minute talk followed by 5-minute Q&A
Demo your Shiny app live
Communicate results to a non-technical audience







To be determined.












Criterion
Weight
Description




Question & Motivation
15%
Is the question clear, relevant, and well-motivated?


Data & Methods
20%
Appropriate data acquisition, cleaning, and analytical methods?


Results & Interpretation
25%
Are results clearly presented and correctly interpreted?


Shiny App
15%
Is the app functional, interactive, and informative?


Communication
15%
Clear delivery, good visuals, appropriate for audience?


Q&A
10%
Thoughtful responses to questions?













ImportantDue Today



Submit the following:

GitHub repository URL – clean, documented, reproducible
Rendered Quarto report (HTML or PDF)
Shiny app – deployed to shinyapps.io or confirmed working
Reflection memo (1 page): What did you learn? What would you do differently? What skill from this course will you use most?







When you’re not presenting:

Be an engaged, respectful audience
Ask at least one question during the Q&A
Take notes – you might learn a technique you can use in your own work





You started the semester with a box of parts. Now you can:

Set up computing infrastructure from scratch
Write R code for data wrangling, visualization, and modeling
Build interactive web applications
Track your work with Git and communicate results with Quarto
Acquire data from files, APIs, and the web
Apply regression, classification, text analysis, spatial analysis, and time series methods
Use AI tools responsibly as coding assistants

That’s cradle-to-grave data science. Well done.

title: Week 3: Data Wrangling, Visualization & AI
text:
This week brings together data manipulation with dplyr, visualization with ggplot2, and the introduction of AI coding assistants. You now have enough R knowledge to start writing real code – and AI tools can accelerate that process when used wisely.










NoteBy the end of this lesson, you will be able to:




Use the core dplyr verbs: filter, select, mutate, summarize, group_by
Use the pipe operator |> to chain operations
Reshape data with tidyr (pivot_longer, pivot_wider)
Build plots with ggplot2 using the grammar of graphics
Use Claude / Copilot as coding assistants
Understand when AI helps vs. when it hallucinates







The pipe takes the output of one function and passes it as the first argument to the next:

# Without pipe
summarize(group_by(filter(mtcars, cyl == 4), gear), avg_mpg = mean(mpg))

# With pipe -- reads left to right, top to bottom
mtcars |>
  filter(cyl == 4) |>
  group_by(gear) |>
  summarize(avg_mpg = mean(mpg))








library(tidyverse)

mtcars |>
  filter(mpg > 25)

mtcars |>
  filter(cyl == 4, gear == 5)





mtcars |>
  select(mpg, wt, cyl)

mtcars |>
  select(-disp, -hp)





mtcars |>
  mutate(kpl = mpg * 0.425,
         heavy = if_else(wt > 3, "Yes", "No"))





mtcars |>
  group_by(cyl) |>
  summarize(
    avg_mpg = mean(mpg),
    sd_mpg = sd(mpg),
    n = n()
  )







# Wide to long
table4a |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(`1999`, `2000`),
               names_to = "year",
               values_to = "cases")

# Long to wide
table2 |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = type,
              values_from = count)





The grammar of graphics: data + aesthetic mappings + geometric objects

# Scatterplot
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Weight vs. MPG by Cylinder Count",
       x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
       y = "Miles per Gallon",
       color = "Cylinders") +
  theme_minimal()




# Histogram
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = mpg)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10, fill = "steelblue", color = "white")

# Boxplot
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl), y = mpg)) +
  geom_boxplot()

# Bar chart
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "darkgreen")

# Line chart
ggplot(economics, aes(x = date, y = unemploy)) +
  geom_line()





ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~cyl) +
  theme_minimal()









Claude (Anthropic) – conversational AI that can write, explain, and debug R code
GitHub Copilot – autocomplete-style AI integrated into your editor





Writing boilerplate code (reading files, basic wrangling)
Explaining error messages
Suggesting ggplot aesthetics and themes
Generating starter code that you then modify
Debugging: paste your error and code, ask what’s wrong





Hallucinated function names or package arguments
Outdated syntax (e.g., %>% vs |>, deprecated functions)
Confidently wrong statistical interpretations
Code that runs but produces incorrect results







WarningThe Rule



AI is a collaborator, not an oracle. You must understand every line of code you submit. If you can’t explain it, you don’t own it.






Be specific: “Write a dplyr pipeline that groups mtcars by cyl and calculates mean mpg” > “analyze mtcars”
Provide context: paste your data structure (glimpse(df)) into the prompt
Iterate: refine the output, don’t accept the first answer blindly
Verify: run the code, check the output, test edge cases









You can wrangle data with dplyr and reshape with tidyr
You can build layered plots with ggplot2
You have AI tools set up and understand their strengths and limits




Week 4: Exploratory Data Analysis

The full EDA workflow: question, wrangle, visualize, communicate
Summary statistics, distributions, correlations, missing data
